proposals.title: Safe Online Adaptation of Diffusion Planners via Constrained Latent Space Fine-Tuning
proposals.motivation: Diffusion models have emerged as powerful planners for autonomous systems, but they are typically trained offline on large, static datasets. This paradigm fails to adapt to novel, out-of-distribution scenarios encountered in real-world deployment, such as rare weather conditions or unseen road geometries. Enabling safe, real-time adaptation is critical for robust autonomy but poses significant challenges in maintaining stability and safety during online learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a diffusion-based planner can be safely adapted online by constraining its fine-tuning updates to a low-dimensional, semantically interpretable latent space, thereby preserving core safe behaviors while efficiently incorporating new experiential data.
proposals.method: We will develop a variational autoencoder (VAE) to learn a compressed latent representation of planned trajectories, building on Rus's prior work on interpretable latent pathways. The diffusion model's denoising network will be conditioned on this latent code. Online adaptation will be performed via a constrained optimization loop that updates only the latent encoder and a small control network, using real-time interaction data. Safety will be enforced through a Lyapunov-based shielding mechanism that projects unsafe latent updates back to a verified safe set.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for safe online adaptation of neural network-based planners, moving beyond the brittle offline training paradigm. It will provide both a theoretical formulation for constrained latent space learning and an empirical validation on autonomous driving simulators, demonstrating improved performance on long-tail scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines the expressivity of diffusion models with the efficiency and interpretability of latent space adaptation, enforcing safety constraints directly in the learning loop rather than as a post-hoc filter, which is non-trivial for generative planners.

proposals.title: Provably Safe Latent-Space Exploration for Autonomous Driving via Lyapunov-Certified Model-Based RL
proposals.motivation: Deploying RL for autonomous driving requires safe exploration during training, a major barrier to real-world application. Pre-2026, model-based RL and Lyapunov-based safety methods have advanced separately, but their integration for complex, high-dimensional perception-action loops remains underexplored. This work addresses the critical need for training frameworks that guarantee safety constraints are never violated while learning from rich sensory inputs.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding a learned dynamics model within a latent, interpretable state space and using it to construct a Lyapunov function, we can enable efficient, provably safe exploration for vision-based autonomous driving policies.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage architecture: 1) A VAE-based perception module (inspired by Rus's prior work) will learn a compact, disentangled latent state representation from high-dimensional input (e.g., camera images). 2) A probabilistic dynamics model will be learned in this latent space. For safe control, we will synthesize a neural network Lyapunov function conditioned on the latent state and train a policy using a model-based RL algorithm (e.g., PETS) constrained by the Lyapunov decay condition, ensuring all exploratory actions remain within a pre-defined safe set.
proposals.contribution: This will yield a novel, end-to-end trainable framework that provides formal safety guarantees during the RL training process for autonomous driving. It will advance the interpretability and safety certification of neural network controllers by tethering them to a stable, model-based latent space.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the co-design of a semantically interpretable latent world model (for efficiency) with a formally verifiable Lyapunov safety filter, creating a unified pipeline for safe RL where the safety guarantees are directly informed by the learned perceptual representation.

proposals.title: Formally Verified Diffusion Policies: Certifying Safety for Autonomous Driving Controllers
proposals.motivation: Diffusion models have emerged as powerful policies for autonomous driving, capable of generating multimodal, smooth control trajectories. However, their iterative denoising process and complex neural network function approximators make them opaque and difficult to assure for safety-critical applications. Pre-2026, formal verification methods for neural networks have advanced for simpler feedforward and control networks, but have not been successfully applied to the sequential, generative nature of diffusion-based control loops.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by embedding semantic-aware constraints into the diffusion model's training loop and leveraging recent advances in neural network verification, we can produce autonomous driving policies with formally certified bounds on output properties, such as adherence to safe steering angles and acceleration limits.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage method. First, we will train a conditional diffusion model for steering and acceleration control, integrating semantic constraints from a Graph Neural Network (GNN) scene encoder (inspired by Rus's prior work) directly into the training loss to shape the latent policy space. Second, we will adapt and apply bounded formal verification techniques, specifically using convex relaxations (e.g., based on CROWN or α-β-CROWN) to the trained denoising network. This will involve unrolling a finite number of denoising steps and verifying that for a given set of input states (e.g., a defined operational design domain), all possible outputs satisfy a formal safety specification.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute the first framework for applying formal verification to diffusion-based control policies, providing a concrete safety certificate for their outputs. It will also deliver a novel, semantically-constrained diffusion model architecture that is more amenable to verification, enhancing both the safety and interpretability of generative driving agents.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct application of neural network verification to the iterative denoising process of a diffusion model, moving beyond verification of static classifiers or simple control networks to assure a generative, sequential decision-making model.

proposals.title: Inferring Implicit Safety Constraints via Diffusion-Based Inverse Optimal Control
proposals.motivation: Autonomous systems require safety constraints for reliable operation, but these constraints are often unknown or difficult to specify manually, especially in complex, unstructured environments. Learning these constraints directly from observed safe trajectories provides a data-driven path to robust control policies, addressing a core challenge in safe autonomy and reinforcement learning.
proposals.hypothesis: A diffusion-based generative model, conditioned on a latent representation of safety, can be trained to solve the inverse problem of inferring the implicit constraints that govern a dataset of safe state trajectories.
proposals.method: We will develop a conditional diffusion model where the denoising network is a graph neural network (GNN) that processes spatio-temporal state sequences. The conditioning variable is a latent constraint embedding inferred by a variational autoencoder (VAE) from safe trajectory demonstrations. The training loop will jointly optimize the VAE and the diffusion model to reconstruct safe trajectories, such that the latent embedding encodes a representation of the underlying safety function. This learned constraint representation can then be used to guide a control policy or validate planned trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, generative framework for constraint inference that is more sample-efficient and interpretable than traditional inverse reinforcement learning. It will contribute a method to extract and formalize implicit safety rules from data, enhancing the safety and interpretability of autonomous driving and robotic systems.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models are used for trajectory generation, their application to the inverse problem of constraint inference, combined with a semantically structured latent space from a VAE, is a novel synthesis. This approach moves beyond predicting actions to explicitly modeling the safety function that governs them.

proposals.title: Neural-Symbolic Task and Motion Planning via Latent Skill Composition and Safety Verification
proposals.motivation: Long-horizon robotic tasks, such as multi-object manipulation in cluttered kitchens, require compositional reasoning over discrete actions and continuous motion. Pre-2026, purely neural approaches struggle with long-term planning guarantees, while classical symbolic planners lack the low-level adaptability for real-world perception and control. A tight integration is needed to scale complex, safe sequential behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding a learned library of verified neural motion primitives within a symbolic planning framework, we can achieve scalable, long-horizon task and motion planning with formal safety guarantees at the motion execution level.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-level architecture. At the high level, a symbolic planner (e.g., PDDL with a learned neural network for precondition/effect prediction) sequences abstract actions. Each action corresponds to a pre-trained, efficient neural network policy (a 'latent skill') from a variational skill autoencoder, inspired by Rus's work on interpretable latent pathways. A novel safety verification loop, using reachability analysis or control barrier functions, will formally check each primitive's execution envelope against dynamic constraints before commitment, ensuring safe composition.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel neural-symbolic TAMP framework with a composable library of verified neural skills, 2) a method for integrating formal safety verification directly into the skill selection loop, and 3) empirical validation on long-horizon robotic manipulation benchmarks, demonstrating improved success rates and safety over end-to-end neural or decoupled approaches.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight, iterative coupling between the symbolic planner and a safety verification module that actively reasons about the executable regions of neural policies, moving beyond treating neural skills as black-box actions or using post-hoc verification.

proposals.title: Uncertainty-Aware Continuous-Time State Estimation via Neural ODEs and Asynchronous Sensor Fusion
proposals.motivation: Robust autonomous systems rely on fusing data from multiple asynchronous sensors (e.g., cameras, LiDAR, IMUs) with highly irregular and sparse sampling rates. Traditional discrete-time filters struggle with this asynchrony, often over-weighting stale measurements during long gaps, leading to overconfident and erroneous state estimates. A principled, continuous-time framework is needed to model information decay and prevent this overconfidence, which is critical for safety in dynamic environments like autonomous driving.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling the system state as a continuous-time process whose uncertainty grows between sensor updates, and fusing asynchronous measurements by conditioning this process with weights inversely proportional to the accumulated uncertainty, will yield more accurate and robust state estimation than discrete-time or fixed-rate fusion methods, especially under sparse and irregular sensor data.
proposals.method: We will model the latent state trajectory (e.g., robot pose and dynamics) using a Neural Ordinary Differential Equation (Neural ODE) to represent a continuous-time prior. A separate uncertainty propagation network, inspired by Kalman filter dynamics, will learn to grow state covariance between updates. Upon receiving an asynchronous measurement, we will perform a conditioning step via a continuous-time Bayesian update rule, where the Kalman gain is modulated by the inverse of the propagated uncertainty specific to that sensor's modality. The system will be trained end-to-end on a multi-sensor autonomous driving dataset using a loss function combining state prediction error and uncertainty calibration metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, end-to-end trainable framework for asynchronous sensor fusion that explicitly accounts for information decay. It will advance the field by delivering more reliable uncertainty quantification for state estimation under real-world sensor constraints. The resulting system will be directly applicable to improving the robustness of autonomous vehicle perception and control pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While Neural ODEs have been used for time-series modeling, their integration with a learned, uncertainty-aware asynchronous fusion mechanism that explicitly penalizes overconfidence from stale data is novel. This moves beyond discrete-time graph-based fusion (like GNNs) and fixed-latency VAEs to a truly continuous-time, uncertainty-calibrated estimator.

proposals.title: Conditional Sensor Fusion via a Modality-Agnostic Latent Representation for Robust Autonomous Perception
proposals.motivation: Current autonomous systems rely on multi-sensor fusion but often use static or late-fusion strategies, which are brittle to individual sensor degradation or adverse environmental conditions. This leads to catastrophic perception failures when a primary sensor (e.g., camera in low light) is compromised. A dynamic, condition-aware fusion mechanism is needed for systems to degrade gracefully, a critical requirement for real-world deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural network that learns a unified, modality-agnostic scene representation, dynamically modulated by a real-time conditioning network analyzing per-sensor signal quality, will yield more robust and interpretable perception that gracefully degrades with sensor failure.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stream architecture. A core encoder network, inspired by variational autoencoder principles for interpretability, ingests raw camera, LiDAR, and radar data to build a latent scene representation. In parallel, a lightweight conditioning network, using temporal convolutions and attention, continuously estimates confidence scores and context vectors from each sensor stream. These condition vectors dynamically gate or weight the contributions of each modality's features within the core encoder via feature-wise linear modulation (FiLM), enabling adaptive fusion. The system will be trained end-to-end on multi-modal datasets (e.g., nuScenes) with synthetically degraded sensor data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel sensor fusion paradigm with explicit resilience to sensor degradation, advancing the safety of autonomous systems. It will also contribute a more interpretable fusion model by analyzing the learned conditioning policies, linking environmental context to sensor reliance.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on attention-based fusion, our method enforces a modality-agnostic core representation that is *dynamically constructed* based on real-time signal quality, moving beyond static weighting or late-fusion ensembles.

proposals.title: Sparse Metric Anchoring: A Robust Correction Network for Scale-Accurate Camera-Centric BEV Perception
proposals.motivation: Camera-centric Bird's Eye View (BEV) perception is a promising, cost-effective paradigm for autonomous driving, but its reliance on monocular depth estimation leads to inherent metric scale ambiguity and drift. This inaccuracy poses a fundamental safety risk for downstream planning and control modules that require globally consistent metric outputs. While dense sensor fusion (e.g., with LiDAR) solves this, it negates the cost advantage, creating a need for a lightweight, robust solution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sparse set of high-confidence, metric range measurements (e.g., from a low-cost, solid-state LiDAR or long-range radar) can serve as 'anchors' to train a neural correction network that continuously calibrates and disambiguates the scale of a monocular BEV perception system, achieving metric accuracy while preserving the camera-first architecture's efficiency.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage neural network. First, a camera-only backbone (e.g., a LSS or transformer-based BEV encoder) generates an initial dense depth and BEV feature map. Second, a lightweight, spatio-temporal graph neural network (GNN) correction module will fuse sparse 3D anchor points from the auxiliary sensor with the BEV features. This GNN will learn to propagate metric corrections across the scene and through time, using a temporal fusion loop (e.g., a BEV Kalman filter or recurrent network) to maintain consistency during anchor sensor dropout. The entire system will be trained end-to-end with a loss combining photometric, geometric, and metric anchor alignment terms.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical BEV perception system that bridges the gap between low-cost camera-only setups and expensive dense sensor suites. It will provide a novel framework for using sparse supervisory signals to correct dense neural geometric models. The resulting robust, scale-accurate perception output will directly enable safer and more reliable downstream planning and control.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on dense sensor fusion or post-hoc scaling, our approach explicitly learns a continuous, context-aware correction function anchored by sparse metric points, and it maintains robustness via a learned fallback mode, which is a novel integration of efficient sensing with neural corrective control.

proposals.title: Adaptive Bird's-Eye-View Perception via Neural Scene Entropy and Hierarchical Mesh Refinement
proposals.motivation: Current autonomous driving perception stacks rely on fixed-resolution Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) grids, which impose a uniform computational cost regardless of scene complexity. This is inefficient, as critical areas like dense urban intersections require fine-grained analysis, while open roads do not. Drawing inspiration from computational physics, we posit that adaptive resource allocation, analogous to Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR) in Computational Fluid Dynamics, can significantly enhance the efficiency and accuracy of BEV-based neural networks.
proposals.hypothesis: A BEV perception system that dynamically adapts its spatial resolution based on real-time, learned measures of scene complexity (e.g., semantic uncertainty, object density) will achieve superior accuracy on downstream tasks (detection, prediction) at a lower computational cost compared to fixed-grid baselines.
proposals.method: We will design a neural network that processes multi-camera input to generate an initial, coarse BEV feature map and a parallel 'refinement field' predicting local entropy or task-relevant error. This field will guide a differentiable, hierarchical BEV transformer architecture, where attention mechanisms operate on a quadtree-like adaptive grid. The refinement criteria will be learned end-to-end with downstream task losses (e.g., 3D detection, motion forecasting), ensuring the grid adapts to optimize final performance. The training loop will utilize efficient dataset sampling strategies to expose the model to diverse complexity scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, adaptive BEV representation framework that dynamically allocates compute, 2) a method for learning refinement criteria directly from task loss, and 3) empirical evidence showing Pareto improvements in the accuracy-efficiency trade-off for autonomous driving perception. It advances the interpretability of resource allocation within neural perception systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses fixed or multi-scale BEV grids, the core novelty is the direct translation of CFD's AMR principle into a learnable, task-driven neural architecture for perception, creating a content-adaptive computational substrate rather than a static one.

proposals.title: Temporally-Guided Sparse Adaptive Pooling for Efficient 4D Bird's-Eye-View Perception
proposals.motivation: Current Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) perception models for autonomous driving rely on dense, frame-by-frame pooling from image features, which is computationally expensive and limits the effective use of long temporal context. As autonomous systems require robust 4D (3D+time) scene understanding for motion forecasting and planning, a fundamental efficiency bottleneck exists in constructing high-resolution BEV representations from multi-camera video streams. This research addresses the critical need for real-time, long-horizon perception that is both accurate and computationally tractable on embedded platforms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-designing temporal fusion with a learned, sparse, and adaptive BEV pooling mechanism, we can achieve state-of-the-art 4D perception performance with significantly reduced computational cost, as the model will learn to focus pooling operations on dynamically salient spatiotemporal regions.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel architecture where a lightweight recurrent network, informed by optical flow and feature entropy from prior frames, predicts a sparse set of 3D sampling locations and their associated importance weights across a temporal window. This 'sampling field' will guide a hardware-aware, adaptive kernel pooling operation that gathers features only from these salient 4D regions. The core temporal fusion will be achieved through a latent pathway, inspired by variational autoencoders for interpretability, that compresses and propagates context. The entire system will be trained end-to-end using a combined loss for 3D detection, motion forecasting, and a sparsity regularizer.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new, efficient paradigm for 4D BEV perception that tightly couples temporal reasoning with adaptive feature sampling, 2) a set of hardware-aware, sparse pooling kernels for real-time deployment, and 3) empirical validation on large-scale autonomous driving datasets (e.g., nuScenes) demonstrating superior accuracy-efficiency trade-offs for long-sequence processing.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the closed-loop, learned prediction of sparse 4D pooling locations based on temporal motion and semantic saliency, moving beyond static, dense pooling or simple frame concatenation, thereby fundamentally re-engineering the BEV construction process for temporal efficiency.

proposals.title: Temporal BEVFusion: A Spatio-Temporal Bird's-Eye View Framework for Robust 3D Perception
proposals.motivation: Current state-of-the-art multi-modal 3D perception models like BEVFusion operate on single frames, limiting their ability to handle occlusions, sensor failures, and motion estimation. For safe autonomous navigation, a system must reason over time to disambiguate static and dynamic objects and improve prediction stability. Incorporating temporal context is a critical next step for robust perception in real-world, dynamic environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending the BEVFusion paradigm with a learned temporal fusion module operating directly on the BEV feature space will significantly improve 3D object detection accuracy, velocity estimation, and robustness to transient occlusions compared to single-frame baselines.
proposals.method: We will extend the BEVFusion architecture by adding a recurrent temporal aggregation network. Features from consecutive LiDAR sweeps and camera images will be lifted to a shared, ego-aligned BEV representation using existing view transformers. A convolutional Gated Recurrent Unit (ConvGRU) or a Transformer-based temporal attention module will then fuse these sequential BEV feature maps. This spatio-temporal BEV feature volume will be the input to the final detection head, enabling the network to learn motion cues and temporal context directly. Training will leverage multi-frame annotations from datasets like nuScenes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a unified, end-to-end trainable framework for multi-modal spatio-temporal perception. It will provide quantitative benchmarks for temporal fusion in BEV and offer insights into how neural networks can leverage history for improved 3D scene understanding and motion forecasting.
proposals.novelty: While prior work stacks point clouds or uses post-hoc tracking, our approach novelly integrates temporal fusion *within* the BEV feature learning loop, allowing for deep, learned motion representations rather than heuristic association. This contrasts with methods that perform late fusion of single-frame detections.

proposals.title: BEVFusion for Multi-Task 3D Scene Understanding: Extending Bird's-Eye View Fusion to Motion Forecasting and Lane Detection
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, robust 3D perception for autonomous vehicles relies on fusing multi-modal sensor data, with BEVFusion establishing a leading paradigm for object detection by unifying camera and LiDAR features in a common bird's-eye-view representation. However, critical downstream tasks like motion forecasting and 3D lane detection, which are essential for safe planning and control, are typically addressed by separate, task-specific models, leading to inefficiencies and potential information loss. A unified, efficient perception system that leverages a shared BEV representation for multiple tasks could significantly enhance the coherence and performance of the full autonomy stack.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dense, geometrically consistent BEV feature space learned by the BEVFusion framework contains rich, generalizable semantic and geometric information that can be effectively leveraged for accurate motion forecasting and 3D lane detection through the addition of lightweight, task-specific prediction heads.
proposals.method: We will adapt the BEVFusion (camera-LiDAR) architecture, using its pre-trained backbone to extract a unified BEV feature map. For motion forecasting, we will append a spatio-temporal graph neural network (GNN) head that operates on detected agent queries from the BEV features to model interactions and predict multi-modal future trajectories. For 3D lane detection, we will integrate a BEV segmentation head with a subsequent polyline fitting module, inspired by works like PersFormer, to directly decode lane structures from the fused BEV features. The entire multi-task network will be trained end-to-end on the nuScenes dataset, employing a weighted sum of detection, forecasting, and lane detection losses.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first demonstration of a single, end-to-end BEVFusion-based model performing joint 3D object detection, motion forecasting, and lane detection, establishing a new benchmark for multi-task efficiency. It will provide a rigorous analysis of the transferability and task-specific sufficiency of BEV features, offering insights into the design of more unified and interpretable autonomous driving systems.
proposals.novelty: While BEVFusion is proven for detection, its application to temporally-aware forecasting and structured lane topology prediction in a unified model is unexplored. The novelty lies in the specific architectural design of the lightweight task heads that effectively decode these distinct outputs from a shared, static BEV feature space without a separate BEV encoder for each task.

proposals.title: BEV-Page: Adaptive Hierarchical Feature Pooling for Efficient Autonomous Driving Perception
proposals.motivation: Bird's Eye View (BEV) pooling is a computational bottleneck in modern autonomous driving perception stacks, requiring the aggregation of high-resolution multi-view image features into a unified BEV representation. This operation is performed repeatedly, often at high frequency, leading to significant latency and energy consumption that hinders deployment on embedded vehicle hardware. Optimizing this core operation is critical for achieving real-time, efficient, and scalable autonomous systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing a BEV pooling mechanism inspired by hierarchical memory paging and caching, we can achieve substantial computational savings with minimal accuracy loss. Specifically, an adaptive system that caches and reuses pooled features for regions of high spatio-temporal locality will outperform static, compute-every-frame approaches in efficiency.
proposals.method: We will design a hierarchical BEV feature manager with a multi-level structure analogous to a page table. A fast, learned cache (analogous to a TLB) will store recently accessed BEV grid features. A gating network, informed by ego-motion, scene semantics, and attention scores, will predict which BEV regions require a full pooling operation from image features and which can be retrieved or interpolated from the cache. The system will be trained end-to-end with a reconstruction loss on the final BEV features and a regularization term penalizing cache misses to balance accuracy and efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, hardware-aware algorithm for efficient BEV perception that directly reduces inference latency and computational load. It will provide a framework for integrating spatio-temporal coherence explicitly into the BEV transformation, moving beyond single-frame optimization. The resulting system will be a step towards deployable, high-performance perception models for autonomous vehicles.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct architectural analogy from virtual memory management to neural feature aggregation, a cross-disciplinary inspiration not previously explored for BEV pooling. While prior work focuses on making the pooling operation itself more efficient, our method introduces an adaptive, hierarchical caching mechanism that dynamically manages computational effort based on predicted feature utility.

proposals.title: BEV-CROSS: Task-Aligned Self-Supervised Pre-training via Cross-View Consistency and Spatial Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) perception models for autonomous driving rely heavily on large-scale, manually annotated datasets for tasks like 3D detection and map segmentation, which are expensive and time-consuming to acquire. While self-supervised pre-training has shown promise in 2D vision, adapting it to the structured, 3D-aware BEV representation remains an open challenge, as generic image-level pretext tasks may not instill the geometric and spatial reasoning priors essential for downstream driving tasks. A method to pre-train a unified BEV encoder on unlabeled multi-camera video could significantly reduce annotation costs and improve model robustness and generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: A self-supervised pre-training curriculum combining explicit cross-view geometric consistency (between images and sparse BEV depth) and spatial reasoning tasks (masked BEV reconstruction) will learn BEV feature representations that are more transferable and yield higher performance on diverse downstream perception tasks compared to training from scratch or using generic image-based pre-training.
proposals.method: We will design a neural network architecture with a shared backbone (e.g., a Swin Transformer) feeding into a view transformer (e.g., LSS or Transformer-based) to generate a unified BEV feature grid. The pre-training uses two core pretext tasks: 1) A cross-view consistency loss that enforces alignment between image features and corresponding simulated sparse depth points projected into the BEV space. 2) A spatial reasoning task where random rectangular regions of the BEV grid—prioritizing areas with dynamic objects (from off-the-shelf 2D detector) and static structures—are masked, and the model must reconstruct the original features using a transformer-based decoder. The training will follow a curriculum, starting with consistency and gradually increasing the masking difficulty.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel self-supervised pre-training framework specifically designed for the BEV representation, demonstrably improving data efficiency and final accuracy on key autonomous driving benchmarks like nuScenes for 3D detection and HD map construction. It will also provide a publicly available pre-trained BEV encoder model to serve as a strong, task-aligned initialization for the research community, reducing the barrier to entry for BEV-based perception research.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior self-supervised work focused on single-image or temporal consistency, our method explicitly designs pretext tasks within the BEV space itself, jointly enforcing geometric grounding from multiple cameras and challenging the model to perform structured, spatial reasoning about both static and dynamic scene elements, which is a direct precursor to downstream perception tasks.

proposals.title: NeRFusion: Continuous Scene Representation for Robust Autonomous Driving via Efficient Neural Radiance Field Fusion
proposals.motivation: Autonomous driving systems rely on discrete, often sparse, sensor observations (e.g., LiDAR point clouds, camera images) which can lead to incomplete scene understanding in complex or occluded environments. Neural Radiance Fields (NeRFs) offer a powerful, continuous scene representation for view synthesis, but their high computational cost and offline training requirements have precluded real-time, dynamic application in robotics. This work addresses the critical need for dense, continuous, and efficient 3D scene models that can be incrementally updated from streaming sensor data for robust perception and planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating a lightweight, dynamically updatable NeRF-like model into a multi-sensor fusion loop, we can synthesize denser, photometrically accurate scene representations that improve downstream tasks like occupancy prediction, path planning, and interpretability for autonomous driving systems.
proposals.method: We propose a novel architecture that fuses features from a semantic-aware Graph Neural Network (inspired by Rus's prior work) with a compact, factorized NeRF variant (like TensoRF or Plenoxels) for efficiency. The system operates in a continuous control loop: a variational autoencoder (VAE) compresses incoming sensor data into a latent space, which is used to conditionally update a persistent, global neural scene representation. This representation is queried in real-time to synthesize novel viewpoints, fill occlusions, and generate a dense semantic occupancy map for the planner.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, efficient framework for online, incremental NeRF construction from a moving autonomous vehicle platform, 2) a method for fusing semantic graph-based features with volumetric radiance fields for improved scene completion, and 3) empirical validation showing that the synthesized continuous representation enhances the robustness of steering estimation and interpretable latent pathway analysis in complex urban scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight integration of an efficient, updatable NeRF model into the real-time perception-control loop of an autonomous system, moving beyond offline scene reconstruction to active, task-driven scene representation that directly informs control and enhances interpretability.

proposals.title: Evidential Multi-Task Networks with Shared Latent Uncertainty for Autonomous Driving
proposals.motivation: Current autonomous driving perception systems often treat tasks like object detection, trajectory prediction, and road segmentation in isolation, leading to inconsistent and poorly calibrated uncertainties. This is a critical safety flaw, as downstream planning and control modules require a unified, reliable measure of confidence across all perceptual inputs. Pre-2026, multi-task learning (MTL) improves efficiency but typically lacks a principled, shared evidential uncertainty framework that can model both task-specific and common-source ambiguities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling a shared latent evidential uncertainty, in addition to task-specific uncertainties, within a neural network will yield more robust, interpretable, and efficient multi-task predictions for autonomous systems, improving both performance and downstream decision-making reliability.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel evidential multi-task architecture building on a shared backbone (e.g., an EfficientNet variant). The model will output parameters for evidential distributions (e.g., Dirichlet for classification, Normal-Inverse-Gamma for regression) for each task. The key innovation is a latent variational autoencoder (VAE) pathway, inspired by Rus's prior work on interpretable latent spaces, that learns a compact representation of scene-level ambiguity. This shared evidential latent variable will condition the task-specific evidence heads, allowing uncertainty to be correlated across tasks. Training will use a combined loss of task-specific negative log-likelihoods and a KL divergence regularizer for the latent pathway.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new MTL architecture with a unified, interpretable uncertainty quantification mechanism, 2) a public benchmark for evidential MTL in autonomous driving using a dataset like nuScenes, and 3) empirical validation showing improved robustness to out-of-distribution inputs and more reliable performance-computation trade-offs compared to disjoint or standard MTL baselines.
proposals.novelty: While evidential deep learning and MTL exist separately, the integration of a learned, shared latent variable to model cross-task epistemic uncertainty is novel. This moves beyond simple parameter sharing or loss weighting to a generative, interpretable model of the underlying causes of uncertainty common to multiple perception tasks.

proposals.title: Evidential Graph Neural Networks with Dynamic Architecture for Uncertainty-Aware Autonomous Driving
proposals.motivation: Current autonomous driving systems often rely on fixed-architecture neural networks, which lack the flexibility to adapt to varying scene complexity and provide reliable uncertainty estimates. This is critical for safety, as overconfident predictions in novel or ambiguous scenarios can lead to failures. Integrating evidential deep learning with modular, dynamically-sized graph neural networks (GNNs) could address both architectural rigidity and uncertainty quantification.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating evidential output layers into a dynamically sized GNN, we can create a system that not only provides calibrated uncertainty estimates for its predictions but also adapts its computational graph structure based on scene complexity, leading to more efficient and reliable perception for autonomous driving.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular GNN where the number of message-passing layers and node/edge features are determined dynamically via a lightweight meta-controller network that processes an initial scene graph. Each node's final representation will be passed through an evidential output layer, parameterizing a Dirichlet distribution over semantic classes to capture epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty. The system will be trained end-to-end on autonomous driving datasets (e.g., nuScenes) using a loss combining evidential marginal likelihood and a sparsity penalty on the meta-controller's architectural decisions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel neural architecture that jointly optimizes for predictive uncertainty and computational efficiency in scene understanding. It will provide a framework for interpretable, adaptable perception models that can signal low confidence in complex scenes, enhancing the safety and robustness of autonomous driving systems.
proposals.novelty: While evidential deep learning and dynamic neural networks exist separately, their integration into a single, adaptive graph-based perception model is novel. This moves beyond simply adding uncertainty outputs to a fixed model, instead co-optimizing the network's structure and its evidential uncertainty estimates based on input.

proposals.title: Closed-Form Liquid Time-Constant Networks for Efficient and Scalable Continuous-Time Learning
proposals.motivation: Liquid Time-Constant (LTC) networks offer a powerful continuous-time, input-dependent neural model with strong expressivity for robotics and control. However, their training relies on computationally expensive numerical ODE solvers, which limits scalability to large datasets and complex architectures. Developing a closed-form solution for the network dynamics would bypass this bottleneck, enabling faster iteration and broader application of these interpretable, neural-inspired models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a mathematically derived closed-form approximation of the LTC network's state dynamics can be constructed, leading to a drastic reduction in training time while preserving the model's core expressive and adaptive properties for sequential and control tasks.
proposals.method: We will derive a closed-form expression for the LTC network's hidden state by leveraging an approximate analytical solution to its governing differential equation, potentially using techniques like variation of parameters or approximating the integral of the input-modulated time constant. This will yield a network layer expressed as a direct, differentiable function of the input history, eliminating the need for an ODE solver in the forward pass. We will implement this as a PyTorch/TensorFlow module and benchmark its training efficiency, scalability, and performance against standard LTC networks on autonomous driving datasets (e.g., nuScenes) and robotic control benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, highly efficient class of continuous-time neural networks that retain the input-adaptive reasoning of LTCs. It will provide the community with a scalable tool for deploying liquid networks on larger-scale problems in autonomy and robotics, and offer new theoretical insights into the solvability of such dynamic systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on improving ODE solver efficiency for neural ODEs, deriving a direct, closed-form function for an input-dependent continuous-time network like the LTC is a novel and non-obvious step that could fundamentally change its training paradigm.

proposals.title: Liquid-Augmented Robustness: Synergistic Integration of Liquid Time-Constant Networks with Adversarial Training for OOD Generalization
proposals.motivation: Deep neural networks remain brittle to out-of-distribution (OOD) shifts, a critical failure mode for safety-critical applications like autonomous driving. While Liquid Time-Constant (LTC) networks offer inherent stability and adaptability through their continuous-time, input-dependent dynamics, and adversarial training explicitly hardens models against worst-case perturbations, their combination is unexplored. This project investigates whether the intrinsic robustness of liquid networks can be systematically amplified by adversarial paradigms for superior OOD performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the synergistic integration of LTC networks with adversarial training will produce models with significantly stronger OOD generalization than either technique alone, as the liquid architecture's stable dynamical system provides a more optimizable and resilient substrate for adversarial robustness objectives.
proposals.method: We will develop a unified training loop for LTC networks that integrates Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) adversarial training. The core architecture will be an LTC-based network, where the continuous-time ODEs are solved using adaptive solvers. The training objective will combine a standard task loss (e.g., steering angle prediction) with an adversarial loss computed on perturbations crafted within an epsilon-bound Lp-norm ball. We will systematically benchmark against baselines: standard LTCs, standard CNNs with adversarial training, and a hybrid using data augmentation (e.g., MixUp) on an LTC, using autonomous driving datasets (e.g., CARLA) with structured OOD splits.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, rigorously evaluated framework for building robust continuous-time learning systems. It will provide new insights into the interplay between architectural inductive biases (liquid dynamics) and optimization-based robustness techniques, advancing the field of reliable machine learning for control. The resulting models are expected to set a new benchmark for OOD generalization in sequential decision-making tasks.
proposals.novelty: While both LTC networks and adversarial training are established, their integration is novel. The non-trivial question is whether the adaptive, stable dynamics of liquid networks fundamentally change the adversarial optimization landscape, potentially mitigating common issues like robust overfitting and enabling more effective robustness transfer to unseen distributional shifts.

proposals.title: Liquid Time-Constant Networks with Topometric Spatial Memory for Long-Horizon Navigation
proposals.motivation: Current autonomous navigation systems often treat perception and planning as separate modules, leading to inefficient, reactive behaviors in novel, large-scale environments. While SLAM provides geometric consistency and Liquid Time-Constant Networks (LTCs) offer efficient, continuous-time perception, their fusion remains unexplored. This gap limits an agent's ability to build and utilize persistent spatial memory for long-term, goal-directed reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a persistent, topometric map from a state-of-the-art visual-inertial SLAM system (e.g., ORB-SLAM3) as a structured, evolving input to a Liquid Time-Constant Network will enable more efficient, robust, and interpretable long-horizon navigation policies compared to end-to-end reactive networks or traditional modular pipelines.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid architecture where a visual-inertial SLAM front-end constructs a persistent pose graph and sparse 3D map. Key elements of this map—agent's pose, nearby landmark descriptors, and subgoal locations—will be encoded as a dynamic graph input to a Liquid Time-Constant Graph Neural Network (LTC-GNN). This LTC-GNN will fuse this spatial memory with real-time sensor streams (e.g., RGB-D) to output control commands. The entire system will be trained end-to-end in simulation using imitation learning from expert trajectories in complex, multi-loop environments, with a loss function that penalizes both control error and map consistency.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel neural architecture that tightly couples continuous-time perception with explicit spatial memory, 2) a benchmark demonstrating improved sample efficiency and long-horizon navigation success in unseen, structured environments compared to baseline methods, and 3) enhanced interpretability via the inspection of the persistent map's influence on the LTC's latent state dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific fusion of explicit, metric-topological SLAM representations with the adaptive, liquid neural dynamics of LTCs, creating a new class of navigation agents that combine the strengths of geometric mapping and efficient continuous-time learning for long-term reasoning.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning Liquid Time-Constant Networks for Few-Shot Visual Environment Adaptation in Autonomous Systems
proposals.motivation: Autonomous systems operating in the open world must rapidly adapt their visual perception to novel environments (e.g., new weather, lighting, or geographic settings). Collecting and labeling large datasets for every new condition is prohibitively slow and expensive. Pre-2026, meta-learning has shown promise for few-shot adaptation, but typically uses standard neural networks with fixed dynamics, which may limit adaptation speed and efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that meta-learning a Liquid Time-Constant (LTC) network will enable faster and more data-efficient adaptation to new visual environments than meta-learning standard architectures, due to the LTC's dynamic, input-dependent neural ODE dynamics providing a richer, more adaptable internal state.
proposals.method: We will employ Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) as the outer-loop algorithm to learn a set of initial parameters for a Liquid Time-Constant network backbone. The inner-loop adaptation will fine-tune these parameters using a very small number of gradient steps on a support set of images from a novel environment. We will benchmark against meta-learned standard CNNs and RNNs on few-shot domain adaptation tasks constructed from autonomous driving datasets (e.g., shifted versions of BDD100K or Carla simulations). The training objective will combine task-specific loss (e.g., steering angle regression) with a complexity regularization term on the LTC's ODE solver.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, meta-learnable architecture combining the fast adaptation of MAML with the continuous-time, input-modulated dynamics of LTC networks, and 2) empirical evidence on robotic vision benchmarks demonstrating superior few-shot adaptation efficiency, measured by both performance after k-shots and the rate of adaptation. This advances the goal of deployable systems that can quickly calibrate to new settings.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning and Liquid Networks are both active pre-2026 research areas, their combination for visual domain adaptation is unexplored. The novelty lies in using meta-learning to explicitly learn the initial liquid dynamics that are most amenable to rapid, few-shot reconfiguration for perception tasks.

proposals.title: Inverting Liquid Policies: A Variational Framework for Environment Inference from Neural Control Networks
proposals.motivation: Understanding the implicit assumptions and operational domain of a trained neural network policy is a critical challenge for safe and interpretable autonomy, especially in vision-based control. Current methods analyze policies through forward simulation or saliency maps, but lack a principled way to answer the inverse question: given only the policy's parameters, what environmental characteristics is it optimized for? This capability would enable rapid policy diagnostics, domain gap analysis, and the generation of informative training environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the synaptic structure and dynamics of a Liquid Time-Constant Network (LTCN) policy encode a latent, disentangled representation of the visual-environmental features critical for its success, and that this representation can be probabilistically inverted to reconstruct a generative model of suitable environments.
proposals.method: We will train a population of LTCN policies on procedurally generated visual navigation environments from datasets like CARLA or DM-Control, where environment characteristics (e.g., road curvature, obstacle density, lighting) are parameterized. We then fix these trained policies and train an inference model—a conditional Variational Autoencoder (cVAE)—to map from the policy's weight matrix and recurrent dynamics to the distribution of environment parameters. The encoder will use graph neural networks to process the policy's connectivity, inspired by semantic-aware GNNs for interpretability. The decoder will generate parameter vectors that can be rendered into sample environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel inverse modeling framework for neural policy analysis, 2) a method to extract a 'capability envelope' for a black-box controller without exhaustive simulation, and 3) a tool for generating counterfactual or adversarial environments that reveal policy brittleness. It directly advances the interpretability of autonomous driving systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses VAEs to find latent pathways *within* a network, we invert the mapping to infer the external world *from* the network. This shifts the focus from explaining internal activations to explaining the policy's implicit design environment, a largely unexplored inverse problem in robot learning.

proposals.title: Neural Implicit Frontier Graphs for Efficient Long-Horizon Autonomous Exploration
proposals.motivation: Current autonomous exploration systems for robots in unknown, large-scale environments rely heavily on geometric frontier detection and hand-engineered utility functions, which scale poorly with environment size and complexity. A data-driven, learning-based approach that can predict high-value exploration targets and learn efficient exploration policies from past experience is crucial for long-duration autonomy in domains like search and rescue or planetary exploration, but remains an open challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neural network architecture combining a Graph Neural Network (GNN) for encoding a robot's sparse frontier graph with a learned implicit neural representation (INR) of the global uncertainty field can generate more efficient, long-horizon exploration policies than classical frontier-based methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage neural architecture. First, a semantic-aware GNN, inspired by Rus's prior work, will process a dynamically built graph of frontier points and their spatial relationships. Second, this graph representation will condition a compact INR (a small MLP) to implicitly model a continuous 'information gain' field across the unexplored map. An efficient training loop will use a model-predictive control (MPC) framework, where the policy selects exploration actions by optimizing over this learned field, using a dataset of simulated exploration episodes for offline training and online fine-tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel neural architecture for coupling discrete frontier graphs with continuous implicit representations for exploration, 2) a scalable, learning-based exploration policy that reduces redundant coverage, and 3) an open-source benchmark and simulated dataset for training and evaluating long-horizon exploration algorithms.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the fusion of explicit graph-based reasoning over frontiers with an implicit neural function approximator to create a globally consistent, learnable utility map, moving beyond local greedy strategies and hard-coded heuristics prevalent in pre-2026 exploration methods.

proposals.title: Liquid State Networks for Visual Self-Dynamics Prediction in Agile Drones
proposals.motivation: Current vision-based drone control systems typically map visual input directly to navigation commands, treating the robot's internal dynamics as a separate, modeled system. This decoupling limits performance in agile, high-speed flight where dynamics are complex and state estimation from traditional sensors (IMUs, GPS) can be delayed or noisy. A method to directly infer a robot's own dynamic state from visual cues could enable faster, more adaptive, and more robust control, especially in GPS-denied or perceptually degraded environments.
proposals.hypothesis: A Liquid Time-Constant Network (LTC), processing a temporal stream of onboard visual data, can directly and accurately predict a drone's immediate future dynamics (e.g., body rates, specific forces) more efficiently and robustly than a system that separates vision-based navigation from a pre-defined dynamic model.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-branch neural architecture. A convolutional encoder will extract spatiotemporal features from a sequence of egocentric images. These features will serve as the continuous-time input to a Liquid Time-Constant Network (LTC), chosen for its proven efficiency in modeling complex temporal dependencies. The LTC's hidden state will be trained via supervised learning to regress to the drone's true dynamics, measured by onboard inertial sensors, using a dataset of aggressive flight maneuvers. A secondary control loop will use these LTC-predicted dynamics as a latent, interpretable state for a model-predictive controller, closing the perception-dynamics-control loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, end-to-end learnable model for visual self-dynamics prediction, demonstrating that vision can serve as a predictive state estimator, not just a scene interpreter; 2) an integrated control framework that uses this latent dynamic prediction for more responsive flight; and 3) empirical validation on a physical drone platform, showing improved agility and robustness compared to baseline decoupled systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses vision for navigation or employs neural networks to approximate known dynamics models, the novelty here is using a liquid network to learn a direct, latent mapping from raw visual input to the robot's own dynamic state, effectively creating a vision-based 'self-dynamics observer' that is co-trained with the control task.

proposals.title: Graph-Aware Dynamic BEV Pooling: A Network-Theoretic Approach to Efficient and Interpretable 3D Scene Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) perception models for autonomous driving rely on fixed, geometry-based pooling operations, which are computationally expensive and semantically naive. They treat all scene regions equally, wasting resources on uninformative areas and potentially breaking semantic object coherence. A method that dynamically adapts computation to the scene's latent information structure is needed for real-time, robust autonomy.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling internal feature activations as a dynamic graph whose structure reflects semantic correlation, we can derive a hardware-aware BEV pooling strategy that sparsifies computation in information-poor regions while preserving detail in critical areas, leading to more efficient and interpretable perception.
proposals.method: We will construct a dynamic graph where nodes are BEV feature vectors and edges are weighted by a learned attention function capturing feature similarity and spatial affinity. Using pre-2026 graph neural network (GNN) techniques and community detection algorithms (e.g., Louvain method), we will identify clusters of semantically related nodes. This graph structure will control a novel dynamic pooling layer, which adjusts kernel density and memory fetch patterns per cluster, prioritizing compute for information-dense communities. The entire model, including the graph construction and adaptive pooling, will be trained end-to-end on existing 3D perception datasets like nuScenes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, network-theoretic framework for understanding and manipulating feature communication in 3D perception models. It will yield a hardware-efficient dynamic pooling operator that reduces FLOPs and memory bandwidth. Furthermore, the learned graph provides an interpretable intermediate representation, linking computational efficiency to scene semantics.
proposals.novelty: While GNNs have been used for scene representation, applying network community analysis to directly control and sparsify core vision operations (like BEV pooling) for hardware efficiency is novel. This fuses graph-based interpretability methods with low-level system-aware optimization in a single, learnable architecture.

proposals.title: Sparse Query Networks: Adaptive Global Inference for Efficient Autonomous Perception
proposals.motivation: Modern vision-based autonomous driving systems rely on dense set prediction architectures like DETR, which use a fixed, large set of object queries to generate a global heatmap of potential detections. This is computationally inefficient for common low-object-density driving scenes (e.g., highways, rural roads), where most queries are wasted on empty regions. As robots and vehicles move towards edge deployment, reducing this fixed computational overhead is critical for real-time performance and energy efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the fixed dense query set with a dynamic, ultra-sparse set generated by a lightweight proposal network will maintain high detection accuracy while significantly reducing computational cost, particularly in scenes with few objects.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage Sparse Query Network (SQN). First, a lightweight, fully convolutional semantic-aware GNN (inspired by Rus's prior work) processes the backbone features to predict a very sparse (<10) set of salient region proposals. Second, these proposals are used as adaptive, content-aware queries for a transformer decoder, replacing the standard dense queries. We will integrate a control loop to dynamically adjust the sparsity based on a learned estimate of scene complexity from the dataset. The training will employ an efficient Hungarian matching loss focused on these sparse queries.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel architecture that decouples query cardinality from model capacity, enabling sub-linear computational scaling with object count. It will provide a practical method for efficient global inference, directly applicable to autonomous driving perception stacks. We will also release a benchmark analyzing the trade-off between sparsity, accuracy, and latency on standard autonomous driving datasets.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on improving attention mechanisms or distillation, our approach fundamentally re-architects the query generation process for global inference, making it adaptive and scene-dependent rather than fixed and dense. This moves beyond pruning or quantizing existing models to a new paradigm for sparse set prediction.

proposals.title: Query-Driven Feature Synthesis: A Fixed-Set Tracking Framework for Efficient and Interpretable Autonomous Perception
proposals.motivation: Current vision-based autonomous systems, particularly those using transformer-based detection heads like DETR, generate object queries from image features. This forward paradigm can be computationally intensive and reactive, as the network must reprocess the entire scene for each frame. For dynamic tasks like tracking, a more efficient and persistent representation of scene entities is needed to reduce redundant computation and improve temporal coherence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that maintaining a fixed, learnable set of persistent tracking queries, which directly synthesize relevant image features via a generative model, will lead to more efficient, stable, and interpretable perception for autonomous driving compared to the standard feature-to-query paradigm.
proposals.method: We propose a novel architecture where a fixed set of object queries, each representing a potential track, is maintained across time. Instead of deriving queries from image features, a conditional generative network (building on VAE or GAN principles) will use each query to synthesize a minimal, task-specific feature representation expected in the current image. These synthesized features will be compared with actual image features via a lightweight matching network (e.g., a semantic-aware GNN) for association and state update. The query set will be updated through a control loop that propagates confident tracks and re-initializes unused queries.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new 'inverse' paradigm for object detection and tracking that prioritizes entity persistence. It aims to significantly reduce per-frame computational cost by focusing synthesis on query-driven regions. Furthermore, by tying interpretable latent variables (the queries) directly to object states over time, it enhances the explainability of the system's perception and tracking decisions.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is inverting the standard query-generation process: features are synthesized from fixed queries, rather than queries from features. This shifts the framework from a per-frame detection model to a persistent, entity-centric tracking model, which is non-obvious within dominant detection-centric architectures.

proposals.title: Neural ODEs for Continuous, Interpretable Trajectory Prediction in Autonomous Driving
proposals.motivation: Autonomous vehicles rely on accurate trajectory prediction of surrounding objects, but discrete-time models struggle with irregular sensor updates and occlusions, leading to unsafe discontinuities. Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (Neural ODEs), introduced pre-2020, offer a principled framework for modeling continuous-time dynamics, which is highly relevant for the physical motion of vehicles and pedestrians. Integrating this with Dr. Rus's recent work on interpretable latent representations for autonomous driving could bridge a critical gap between continuous modeling and system transparency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a latent-variable model combining a Neural ODE for continuous-time dynamics with a structured variational autoencoder (VAE) for interpretable state representation will yield more accurate, robust, and explainable trajectory predictions compared to discrete recurrent or graph-based models, especially under irregular observation schedules.
proposals.method: We will develop a model where a VAE encoder maps observed trajectory snippets to a latent space disentangled into semantic factors (e.g., intent, kinematics). The core dynamics in this latent space will be governed by a Neural ODE, whose derivative network is a compact, efficient neural network. This continuous latent state can be queried at any future time for prediction. The training loop will combine a reconstruction loss on observed points, a KL divergence for the VAE, and an auxiliary control-inspired loss (e.g., on latent derivatives) to encourage physically plausible dynamics, trained on large-scale autonomous driving datasets like nuScenes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel trajectory prediction model that provides continuous-time, uncertainty-aware forecasts, enhancing robustness to sensor dropouts. It will also advance the interpretability of autonomous driving systems by generating human-understandable, continuous latent pathways that explain an object's predicted motion, directly extending Dr. Rus's prior work on interpretable latent spaces.
proposals.novelty: While Neural ODEs have been applied to time-series, their integration with structured, interpretable latent spaces for physical trajectory modeling in autonomous driving is novel. This approach moves beyond using Neural ODEs as mere black-box function approximators by embedding them within a semantically-aware architecture designed for control and safety-critical interpretation.

proposals.title: Controllable Video Synthesis from Object Tracks via a Disentangled Neural Scene Representation
proposals.motivation: Autonomous systems require robust world models that can predict and simulate complex multi-agent dynamics. Current video prediction models often lack explicit, interpretable control over individual scene elements, limiting their utility for planning and verification. This work addresses the need to generate physically plausible video sequences conditioned on high-level, object-centric specifications like bounding box tracks.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging a disentangled, object-centric latent representation learned from raw video, a generative model can synthesize realistic and temporally consistent video sequences that adhere to user-provided object trajectories, enabling direct control over scene composition.
proposals.method: We will extend a variational autoencoder (VAE) framework, informed by Rus's prior work on interpretable latent pathways. The encoder will decompose a video sequence into a set of object-specific latent codes (for appearance) and explicit trajectory variables (for position). A conditional video generator, based on a recurrent network or a temporal GNN, will take these disentangled latents and a set of target future tracks as input. It will learn to render the corresponding video frames through an adversarial training loop on a large-scale autonomous driving dataset (e.g., nuScenes).
proposals.contribution: This will produce a novel model for controllable video generation from sparse inputs, directly useful for simulation and data augmentation in robotics. It will also advance the interpretability of neural scene representations by demonstrating that object tracks can serve as an effective control interface for a generative model.
proposals.novelty: While video prediction and track prediction are often separate tasks, this work uniquely inverts the process, using tracks as the input control signal for a generative model. It combines the object-centric representation learning from Rus's group with conditional video synthesis in a novel, controllable pipeline.

proposals.title: Pathway-Aware Distillation: Enhancing RFAD for Deep Finite-Width Networks via Latent Space Decomposition
proposals.motivation: Dataset distillation methods like RFAD (Recursive Fisher Approximation for Dataset Distillation) enable efficient training from small synthetic coresets, but their performance degrades for deeper, finite-width networks due to the loss of critical feature hierarchies and activation pathways during compression. This limits their application in resource-constrained robotics and autonomous systems where deploying deep, compact networks is essential. Improving distillation for such architectures is crucial for enabling lifelong learning and efficient control policies from minimal data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling and preserving the latent functional pathways of a deep teacher network within the synthetic coreset will significantly improve the training stability and final performance of deep, finite-width student networks distilled via RFAD.
proposals.method: We will augment the RFAD framework with a variational autoencoder (VAE) trained on the teacher network's intermediate activations to learn a disentangled latent space of functional pathways. During coreset synthesis, we will incorporate a regularization term that enforces alignment between the student's and teacher's latent pathway distributions. This will be integrated into the recursive Fisher approximation loop, using a graph neural network (GNN) to model dependencies between pathways across layers, ensuring the synthetic data supports the complex feature evolution in deep, finite-width architectures.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust dataset distillation technique specifically tailored for deep, finite-width networks, expanding the utility of coreset-based training to more complex models. It will provide new insights into the relationship between latent network representations and distillation efficacy. The resulting method will enable more efficient training of compact, interpretable neural controllers for autonomous systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating a pathway-aware, generative latent model directly into the RFAD optimization loop to address depth-specific distillation failures, a combination not explored in prior dataset or knowledge distillation literature.

proposals.title: Dual-Knowledge Distillation: Efficiently Compressing Large Models via Dataset-Aware Policy Learning
proposals.motivation: Deploying large, high-performance neural networks on resource-constrained platforms like autonomous vehicles remains a critical challenge. While knowledge distillation (KD) is a standard compression technique, it often requires a curated, representative dataset, which can be expensive or infeasible to obtain. This work addresses the dual problem: leveraging the original, large-scale training dataset to distill a compact model, bypassing the need for specialized distillation data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distillation policy, learned jointly with the student network, can selectively query the original large dataset to identify maximally informative samples for transferring the teacher's function, leading to a more efficient and higher-fidelity compressed model than standard KD or dataset distillation methods.
proposals.method: We propose a bi-level optimization framework. The outer loop trains a lightweight policy network (a small GNN or MLP) that takes intermediate feature representations from both teacher and student as input and outputs a sampling weight for each training batch. The inner loop uses these weights to perform weighted knowledge distillation (using both logit and intermediate feature matching losses) on the original dataset to update the student. The policy is trained via meta-gradients to maximize the student's validation performance, creating a dataset-aware distillation loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, dataset-efficient model compression algorithm that eliminates the distillation data bottleneck. It will provide new insights into the interplay between dataset structure and knowledge transfer, particularly for autonomous systems where interpretable, efficient control networks are essential.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard KD or dataset distillation, which compresses either the model or the data, our method learns a dynamic sampling policy over the original dataset, making the distillation process itself adaptive and data-aware—a non-obvious dual to conventional approaches.

proposals.title: Direct World Model Learning from Real-World Driving Data via Latent Dynamics and Semantic Graph Networks
proposals.motivation: Training autonomous agents in high-fidelity simulators is computationally expensive and suffers from the sim-to-real gap, where models fail to generalize to the complexities of the physical world. A method to learn a predictive world model directly from heterogeneous, real-world sensor data would bypass this reliance, enabling more robust and sample-efficient policy learning for robotics and autonomous driving.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a world model combining a structured latent dynamics model with a semantic-aware graph neural network can be trained end-to-end on offline real-world driving datasets to accurately predict future states and rewards, enabling effective policy training without a simulator.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stream architecture. First, a variational autoencoder (VAE) will compress high-dimensional input (e.g., camera, lidar) into a latent state. Second, a Graph Neural Network (GNN), informed by semantic scene graphs (objects, lanes), will model the stochastic transition dynamics in this latent space, as inspired by Rus's prior work on semantic GNNs and latent pathways. The model will be trained on datasets like nuScenes using a temporal contrastive loss for representation learning and a maximum likelihood objective for dynamics prediction, forming a complete environment model for downstream policy optimization via algorithms like Dreamer.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for simulator-free, data-driven world model learning, specifically for autonomous systems. It will advance the interpretability and data efficiency of model-based reinforcement learning by grounding dynamics in semantic scene structure, directly applicable to autonomous driving control.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses GNNs for perception or VAEs for latent spaces, the integration of a semantic GNN as the core dynamics model within a world model, trained entirely on offline real-world data without simulator supervision, is a novel synthesis for direct real-world agent training.

proposals.title: Graph-Based Multi-Agent Imitation Learning for Competitive and Collaborative Robotic Racing
proposals.motivation: Current autonomous driving research, including Dr. Rus's work on interpretable latent spaces and steering estimation, focuses predominantly on single-agent scenarios. Real-world applications like autonomous racing and warehouse logistics inherently involve multi-agent interactions, where competitive and collaborative behaviors must be learned from complex, high-dimensional sensory input. Extending single-agent frameworks to multi-agent settings is a critical, unsolved challenge for deploying robust robotic systems in shared dynamic environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a graph neural network (GNN) architecture, trained via decentralized imitation learning on a multi-agent trajectory dataset, can effectively model the latent interaction dynamics between agents, enabling emergent competitive racing and collaborative task behaviors that outperform rule-based or centralized multi-agent controllers.
proposals.method: We will extend the semantic-aware GNN framework from prior steering work to a multi-agent graph, where nodes are agents with proprioceptive features and edges represent spatial or interaction-aware connections. A variational autoencoder (VAE) structure will be integrated to learn a compressed, interpretable latent representation of the multi-agent state, similar to the 'Exploring Latent Pathways' paper but for joint action spaces. Training will use a decentralized behavioral cloning objective on a new multi-vehicle racing dataset, with a novel attention mechanism in the GNN to dynamically weight the influence of neighboring agents. A closed-loop simulation environment (e.g., based on LFM2 principles) will serve for evaluation and control policy deployment.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel GNN-VAE architecture for decentralized multi-agent imitation learning, 2) a publicly available dataset of multi-agent racing trajectories, and 3) empirical validation showing the framework's superiority in sample efficiency and emergent behavior complexity over baseline multi-agent reinforcement learning and centralized methods in both competitive and collaborative tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of an interpretable latent dynamics model (VAE) with a graph-based interaction model specifically for decentralized control in mixed-motive multi-robot tasks, moving beyond single-agent perception or purely cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning benchmarks.

proposals.title: Generalizable World Models via Disentangled and Compositional Observation Networks
proposals.motivation: Model-based reinforcement learning agents often fail to generalize to novel environments due to brittle observation models that overfit to spurious correlations in training data. This is a critical bottleneck for deploying reliable autonomous systems, such as self-driving cars, in the open world. Improving the generalization of the perception-to-state mapping is a foundational step toward more robust and sample-efficient agents.
