proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an observation model explicitly architected to disentangle latent state factors and support compositional reasoning over object-centric entities will yield significantly improved generalization in model-based control, compared to standard monolithic encoder architectures.
proposals.method: We will design a novel observation network that integrates a slot-attention mechanism to decompose a scene into object-centric representations. These slots will be processed by a small, relational graph neural network (GNN) to model interactions, inspired by Rus's prior work on semantic-aware GNNs. The disentangled, structured latent state will then serve as input to a compact dynamics model and policy network. We will train and evaluate this agent using a loop of control in procedurally generated environments (e.g., Crafter, MiniGrid) to test generalization to novel object configurations and dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, interpretable architecture for observation models that promotes compositional generalization. It will provide empirical evidence linking disentangled, object-centric latent spaces to improved robustness in model-based planning, advancing the field beyond black-box perception modules.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of a slot-attention-based perceptual front-end with a relational GNN for state refinement, specifically tailored for generalization within a model-based RL loop, moving beyond purely reconstruction-focused latent variable models.

proposals.title: World Models as a Learned Simulator: Bridging Sample Efficiency and Deployment Performance with Model-Free Fine-Tuning
proposals.motivation: Deploying reinforcement learning (RL) in the real world, such as for autonomous driving, is severely constrained by sample inefficiency and safety. World models offer sample-efficient training in a learned latent dynamics model, but can suffer from compounding error and suboptimal asymptotic performance. A hybrid approach is needed to leverage the data efficiency of world models while achieving the high task performance of model-free RL in the target domain.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pre-training a compact world model on limited real-world interaction data, followed by fine-tuning a model-free policy within this learned simulator and then on real sensory input, will yield superior final task performance and robustness compared to purely model-based or model-free baselines.
proposals.method: We will first train a variational world model (e.g., a Recurrent State-Space Model) on a modest dataset of real-world driving sequences to learn a compressed, temporally consistent latent dynamics function. This model will serve as an efficient, differentiable simulator. We will then use this simulator to train a model-free policy (e.g., Soft Actor-Critic) via latent-space RL, exploiting the model's efficiency. Finally, this pre-trained policy will be fine-tuned directly on the real-world control loop with a small amount of additional on-policy data, using the world model to provide auxiliary reconstruction or dynamics prediction losses for regularization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete framework for combining the strengths of model-based and model-free RL in real-world robotic control. It will demonstrate a measurable performance boost in a challenging domain like autonomous steering, while maintaining the interpretability benefits of a latent world model. The resulting methodology will offer a practical path toward deploying sample-efficient RL agents that continue to improve with real-world experience.
proposals.novelty: While world models and model-free RL are well-studied, the key novelty lies in the specific, staged integration where the world model transitions from a primary training environment to a regularizer during real-world fine-tuning, creating a synergistic 'learned simulator' pipeline rather than a simple ensemble or planning tool.

proposals.title: Bayesian World Models for Robust Sim2Real Transfer in Autonomous Driving
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in scaling autonomous driving is the sim2real gap, where policies trained in simulation fail due to inaccurate world model predictions. Pre-2026, world models like those in DreamerV2 or PlaNet provide deterministic predictions, lacking a measure of their own uncertainty. This leads to overconfident and brittle policies when encountering out-of-distribution real-world scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating Bayesian Neural Networks (BNNs) into the latent dynamics model of a world-model-based RL agent will provide reliable uncertainty estimates, enabling the agent to identify and adaptively handle states where simulation predictions are unreliable, thereby significantly improving sim2real robustness.
proposals.method: We will extend a state-of-the-art world model (e.g., Dreamer) by replacing its deterministic latent transition network with a Bayesian Neural Network using Monte Carlo Dropout or a variational inference-based BNN. The agent's policy and value networks will be conditioned on both the latent state and the predictive uncertainty (e.g., variance) from the BNN. Training will involve a two-stage process: first, training the Bayesian world model on a large, diverse simulated driving dataset; second, fine-tuning the policy using RL, where uncertainty is used to modulate exploration or trigger safe, fallback control loops in high-uncertainty states.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, uncertainty-aware world model architecture for autonomous driving. It will provide a concrete method for quantifying prediction uncertainty in model-based RL, leading to more interpretable and cautious agent behavior. The primary evaluation will demonstrate improved zero-shot sim2real transfer and reduced failure rates in real-world driving scenarios compared to deterministic baselines.
proposals.novelty: While BNNs have been explored in perception, their integration into the sequential latent dynamics of a world model for RL-based driving control is novel. This approach moves beyond post-hoc uncertainty estimation to actively using epistemic uncertainty within the agent's decision-making loop for sim2real adaptation.

proposals.title: Contrastive Pre-training of Latent Representations for Efficient LiDAR-Based Autonomous Driving
proposals.motivation: Autonomous driving systems rely heavily on LiDAR data, but its high-dimensional, unstructured nature makes learning efficient and generalizable representations challenging. Current supervised methods require massive, expensively labeled datasets, which limits scalability and adaptability to new environments. Self-supervised learning offers a path to leverage vast unlabeled LiDAR streams, but effective frameworks for this complex 3D modality are underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a contrastive self-supervised learning objective, designed specifically for sequential LiDAR point clouds, can learn rich, task-agnostic latent representations that significantly improve sample efficiency and downstream performance in autonomous driving tasks like object detection and motion forecasting.
proposals.method: We will develop a contrastive learning framework where positive pairs are generated from temporally adjacent LiDAR sweeps in a driving dataset, exploiting the inherent spatial coherence of sequential observations. Negative pairs will be formed from spatially or temporally distant sweeps. The core architecture will be a Siamese network built upon efficient, sparse 3D convolutional encoders (e.g., MinkowskiEngine) to process point clouds. We will employ the NT-Xent loss (InfoNCE) to train the encoder to map positive pairs closer in a latent space, pre-training on large-scale unlabeled datasets like nuScenes or KITTI.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a pre-trained LiDAR encoder that provides robust, transferable features, reducing the labeled data required for downstream autonomous driving tasks. It will also contribute a benchmark for self-supervised learning on sequential LiDAR data, evaluating the learned representations on tasks like 3D object detection and semantic segmentation.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning has succeeded in images, its tailored application to raw, sequential LiDAR point clouds for autonomous driving is novel. The key innovation is the design of a temporal contrastive pretext task that leverages the natural structure of driving data, moving beyond simple geometric augmentations used in static 3D contrastive learning.

proposals.title: Imagination-Augmented Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Strategic Autonomous Racing
proposals.motivation: Autonomous racing demands real-time, strategic decision-making under extreme dynamics and partial observability. Pre-2026, end-to-end RL approaches struggle with sample inefficiency and lack of interpretable high-level strategy, while classical hierarchical methods often rely on handcrafted models or sub-goals. A data-driven, imagination-based hierarchical framework is needed to enable agents to learn complex, long-horizon racing tactics like overtaking and defensive blocking from raw sensory input.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical agent, consisting of a high-level planner trained with model-based RL in a learned latent world model and a low-level controller trained with model-free RL to execute sub-policies, will achieve superior sample efficiency and strategic complexity in autonomous racing compared to monolithic or model-free baselines.
proposals.method: We propose a two-level architecture. The high-level planner operates at a coarse temporal scale, using a learned latent dynamics model (e.g., a Variational Autoencoder or RSSM) as its 'imagination' module to predict outcomes of potential strategic sequences (e.g., 'draft then overtake'). It outputs abstract sub-goals. The low-level controller, a separate neural network policy, receives these sub-goals and raw sensor input (or semantic features) and outputs precise control commands. Both levels are trained jointly using a combination of Dreamer-like model-based RL for the planner and TD3 or SAC for the low-level controller, all within the imagined latent space to ensure efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, fully trainable hierarchical RL framework for autonomous racing that decouples strategy from control. It will demonstrate improved sample efficiency and emergent complex strategies in high-fidelity simulators (e.g., Gran Turismo Sport). The work will also advance the interpretability of autonomous driving agents by making the latent strategic plan an explicit, analyzable component of the system.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight integration of a learned world model ('imagination') to train *both* the high-level strategic planner and the low-level controller in a single, end-to-end differentiable hierarchy, moving beyond using imagination only for low-level skill learning or using fixed hierarchies.

proposals.title: Latent Imagination for Real-World Autonomous Vehicle Control: Bridging the Simulation-to-Reality Gap
proposals.motivation: Deploying robust autonomous driving systems requires extensive real-world testing, which is costly, time-consuming, and potentially unsafe. While model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) with latent dynamics models (e.g., Dreamer) shows promise for efficient training in simulation, a critical gap exists in applying such 'latent imagination' frameworks to control full-scale vehicles on complex, real roads. This work addresses the core challenge of transferring a learned latent world model from curated datasets to safe, real-time control under noisy, high-dimensional sensor input.
proposals.hypothesis: A latent dynamics model, trained offline on large-scale, real-world driving datasets, can be integrated with a model-predictive control (MPC) loop to enable safe and interpretable autonomous driving by planning in a learned, compact latent state space, thereby reducing the reality gap inherent in pure simulation-to-real transfer.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture, training a Recurrent State-Space Model (RSSM) on the nuScenes or Waymo Open Dataset to learn a latent dynamics function from high-dimensional camera and LiDAR input. The key innovation is a hybrid control loop: a low-level safety controller (e.g., a proven rule-based or robust MPC layer) will execute short-horizon trajectories planned by a high-level latent MPC planner. This planner will perform online 'imagination' by rolling out multiple latent trajectories, decoding them to predicted semantic occupancy grids (inspired by Rus's prior work on interpretability) for cost evaluation, and selecting the optimal action sequence. We will implement and test this system in a full-scale vehicle platform, initially in closed-course environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel, hybrid MBRL architecture for real-world autonomous vehicle control that marries the sample efficiency of latent imagination with robust safety guarantees, 2) empirical validation on a full-scale vehicle, demonstrating a path to deployable MBRL, and 3) enhanced interpretability through the use of semantically decoded latent rollouts for planner decision analysis.
proposals.novelty: While latent world models are established in simulation, their application for online planning and control of a full-scale autonomous vehicle on real roads is unexplored. The novelty lies in the integration of a learned latent dynamics model with a real-time, safety-critical MPC loop and its validation on a physical system, moving beyond offline prediction or simulated control.

proposals.title: Inverse Environment Design via Differentiable World Models for Targeted Agent Proficiency
proposals.motivation: Current autonomous system training relies on static or procedurally generated environments, which are not optimized to efficiently teach specific skills or diagnose failure modes. A method to algorithmically design challenging scenarios would accelerate training and robustness testing, particularly for safety-critical applications like autonomous driving. This addresses a key bottleneck in developing reliable agents by moving from passive data collection to active, curriculum-driven environment synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: A differentiable world model, trained on agent-environment interaction data, can be inverted through gradient-based optimization to generate novel environmental parameters (e.g., track layouts) that maximize a specified objective, such as exposing a specific agent weakness or training for a targeted skill.
proposals.method: We will train a latent dynamics model (e.g., a Variational Autoencoder or Recurrent State-Space Model) on sequences of agent states, actions, and environmental parameters. This model will be made differentiable with respect to a parameterized track representation (e.g., a spline). We will then formulate an inverse optimization loop that uses gradient ascent on these parameters to maximize a cost function defined on the agent's predicted behavior, such as its expected control effort, divergence from a nominal path, or likelihood of a safety violation. Techniques from differentiable simulation and neural adjoint methods will be integrated to ensure efficient backpropagation through the world model.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for automatic and interpretable test scenario generation, directly linking agent proficiency to environmental design. It will provide a tool for creating adaptive training curricula and rigorous stress-testing suites, moving beyond hand-crafted benchmarks. The core algorithm will be a step towards closing the loop between learning agents and their training environments.
proposals.novelty: While world models are used for prediction and planning, inverting them for purposeful environment design is an underexplored inverse problem. The approach uniquely combines the interpretable latent spaces from Dr. Rus's prior VAE work with gradient-based optimization to *control* the training environment, not just the agent, which is a departure from standard reinforcement learning and dataset augmentation methods.

proposals.title: Dual-Objective World Models: Imitation and Imagination for Sample-Efficient Autonomous Driving Policy Learning
proposals.motivation: Current reinforcement learning (RL) approaches for autonomous driving require vast, often unsafe, on-road exploration to learn effective policies. While world models can reduce this need by learning in imagination, they are typically trained solely on RL agent data, which is initially poor. Leveraging abundant datasets of expert human demonstrations offers a rich, safe source of high-quality driving behavior, but current imitation learning methods lack the long-horizon reasoning of model-based planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a world model jointly on human demonstration data and agent interaction data will enable more sample-efficient and robust policy learning, as the model will learn to accurately imagine consequences from both optimal and exploratory states.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-objective variational world model (e.g., a Recurrent State-Space Model) trained with a composite loss. The first objective is a behavioral cloning loss on expert state-action trajectories. The second is a standard model-based RL objective on agent-collected data. The trained model will then be used within a Model Predictive Control (MPC) planner or to train a policy via imagined rollouts. We will integrate semantic scene graphs (inspired by Rus's prior GNN work) as a structured input representation to improve generalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a unified framework for combining imitation and model-based RL, demonstrating significant improvements in sample efficiency and final policy performance on autonomous driving benchmarks. It will also provide insights into how demonstration data shapes the learned latent dynamics of a world model, enhancing its interpretability and safety.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the dual training of a single world model on both demonstration and exploration data, moving beyond using demonstrations merely for behavioral cloning or to initialize a policy. This allows the planner to 'imagine' from expert contexts, a capability not central to prior model-based imitation or RL methods.

proposals.title: Closed-Form Continuous-Time Neural Networks for Efficient and Interpretable Robotic Control
proposals.motivation: Modern deep learning models for robotics, such as those used in autonomous driving, often rely on discrete-time, black-box neural networks that are computationally expensive and difficult to interpret. Recent work on Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (Neural ODEs) has introduced continuous-time models but suffers from slow training due to numerical ODE solvers. A more efficient and architecturally explicit continuous-time formulation is needed for real-time control systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By deriving a closed-form, analytical solution to the underlying continuous-time dynamics of a neural layer, we can construct novel network architectures that are inherently more parameter-efficient, faster to train and evaluate, and yield more interpretable latent state trajectories for control tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a new layer architecture based on the explicit solution to a linear time-invariant (LTI) system, parameterizing the state transition matrix. This Closed-Form Network (CFN) layer will be integrated into a graph-based or sequential model for processing temporal sensor data. We will apply these models to autonomous vehicle steering prediction and low-level robotic control, benchmarking against standard RNNs, Transformers, and Neural ODEs on metrics of accuracy, training time, inference speed, and trajectory smoothness.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a new class of efficient, continuous-time neural layers with guaranteed stability properties, 2) a framework for more interpretable latent pathway analysis in control systems, aligning with Rus's prior work on interpretable autonomy, and 3) empirical validation on real-world robotic datasets demonstrating superior computational efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While Neural ODEs learn a vector field for numerical integration, our approach directly encodes dynamics into the network architecture via a parameterized closed-form solution, eliminating the need for iterative ODE solvers and enabling direct architectural constraints for stability and efficiency.

proposals.title: Closed-Form Dynamics for Efficient Spatiotemporal Representation Learning in Robotic Control
proposals.motivation: Autonomous systems require robust spatiotemporal representations for tasks like navigation and manipulation, but training deep recurrent or graph networks on these tasks is computationally intensive and data-hungry. This limits real-time adaptation and interpretability in safety-critical applications like autonomous driving, a core focus of Rus's recent work. A more efficient, physics-aware learning paradigm is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating fast, closed-form analytical models of physical dynamics (e.g., for vehicle kinematics or object motion) as a structured prior within a neural network training loop will yield more data-efficient, interpretable, and generalizable spatiotemporal representations for decision-making.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid architecture where a primary neural network (a Graph Neural Network or Transformer) processes high-dimensional sensory input (e.g., lidar, camera). Its latent representations will be regularized by a parallel, differentiable closed-form model (e.g., a kinematic bicycle model) that operates on a lower-dimensional state abstraction. This will be enforced via a novel training objective combining task-specific loss with a consistency loss between the neural network's predicted dynamics and the closed-form model's projections, creating an efficient, interpretable latent pathway.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for sample-efficient and physically-grounded representation learning. It will advance the interpretability of autonomous driving systems by providing clear linkages between latent states and known dynamics. The method aims to set a new benchmark for training efficiency on spatiotemporal control tasks.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid modeling exists, the novelty lies in using closed-form models not just for simulation, but as a direct, trainable regularizer for the latent space of a deep network, explicitly shaping the learned spatiotemporal representations for control. This moves beyond post-hoc interpretation to built-in physical structure.

proposals.title: Efficient Spatiotemporal Learning via Latent Graph Neural Networks and Adaptive Dataset Curation
proposals.motivation: Autonomous systems and sensor networks generate petabytes of spatiotemporal data, but training on such datasets is prohibitively slow and computationally expensive, limiting model scale and performance. Pre-2026 methods for video and sensor stream analysis often rely on monolithic architectures that do not efficiently exploit the inherent structure and redundancy in large-scale spatiotemporal signals. This bottleneck prevents the exploration of richer, more complex world models necessary for robust autonomy.
proposals.hypothesis: By combining a latent variational graph neural network (GNN) architecture with an adaptive, efficiency-driven dataset curation loop, we can achieve orders-of-magnitude training speedup on massive spatiotemporal datasets without sacrificing model accuracy or interpretability.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, a variational autoencoder (VAE) will compress high-dimensional video or sensor network inputs into a sparse latent graph representation, where nodes are semantic features and edges capture spatiotemporal relationships, building on prior work in semantic-aware GNNs. Second, we will implement a novel control loop that actively curates the training dataset by prioritizing informative and diverse samples based on latent space dynamics and training loss gradients, drastically reducing redundant computations. Training will leverage efficient network libraries (e.g., JAX, PyTorch Geometric) optimized for distributed graph processing.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable training framework for spatiotemporal data, enabling the use of previously intractable dataset sizes. It will advance the interpretability of large-scale models by enforcing a structured latent graph. The resulting methods will be validated on autonomous driving video streams and distributed environmental sensor networks, providing concrete benchmarks for the community.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a latent VAE-GNN for compression with an adaptive control loop for dataset curation creates a synergistic efficiency gain not explored in prior work. This moves beyond simple model pruning or distributed training to a holistic, data-and-model co-design approach for spatiotemporal learning.

proposals.title: Inverse Dynamics Inference: Recovering Neural Network Parameters from Closed-Form Control Solutions
proposals.motivation: In robotics and autonomous systems, neural networks are often trained to approximate complex control policies or system dynamics. However, these networks are typically black boxes, making it difficult to interpret their learned parameters or verify their alignment with known physical models. An inverse approach, inferring the network's internal synaptic weights or architectural priors from its observed closed-form output behavior, could enable model introspection, safety verification, and more efficient transfer learning, especially in safety-critical domains like autonomous driving.
proposals.hypothesis: Given a known, analytically tractable closed-form solution (e.g., a steering control function) and a dataset of its input-output mappings, the underlying parameters of a constrained neural network architecture that approximates this solution can be uniquely inferred through a structured inverse optimization problem.
proposals.method: We will formulate the inference as a constrained optimization problem, minimizing the discrepancy between the network's output and the closed-form solution across a dataset. The network architecture will be constrained using semantic-aware graph neural network (GNN) layers, informed by pre-2026 work on interpretable latent spaces (e.g., VAEs), to impose structural priors on the parameter space. We will employ efficient, second-order optimization techniques suitable for inverse problems and design a training loop that alternates between validating the forward pass and refining the parameter inference, ensuring stability and convergence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for the interpretability and verification of neural networks used in control systems. It will contribute a method to 'reverse-engineer' a network's functional representation, bridging the gap between data-driven learning and model-based reasoning. The resulting techniques could enhance the safety analysis and certification of autonomous systems by linking network behavior to known, verifiable dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While most research focuses on the forward problem of learning functions from data, this proposal inverts the paradigm to infer network parameters from a known function. This approach is distinct from standard network pruning or distillation, as it seeks to recover the specific synaptic parameters that generate a given global input-output map, not just a simplified behavioral clone.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Safe Planning with Neural Barrier Functions for Long-Horizon Task and Motion Planning
proposals.motivation: Long-horizon task and motion planning (TAMP) for robots in unstructured environments remains a significant challenge, particularly in ensuring safety over extended sequences of actions. Pre-2026, methods often separate high-level symbolic planning from low-level safe control, leading to potential safety violations during execution or computationally expensive monolithic verification. Integrating formal safety guarantees into scalable hierarchical planning is a critical open problem for deploying autonomous systems in complex, dynamic settings.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating differentiable BarrierNet layers—which encode control barrier function (CBF) constraints directly into a neural network—within a hierarchical planning framework, we can achieve scalable, long-horizon TAMP with provable safety guarantees at every level of abstraction.
proposals.method: We will develop a three-level architecture: 1) A high-level task planner (e.g., a learned neural network or symbolic planner) generates a sequence of sub-goals. 2) A mid-level neural motion planner, structured as a Graph Neural Network (GNN) informed by semantic scene graphs, refines these into trajectories. 3) At the lowest level, BarrierNet modules, integrated into the GNN's node update functions, will continuously filter planned trajectories to satisfy CBF-based safety constraints. The entire system will be trained end-to-end on a synthetic dataset of long-horizon tasks, using a loss function that balances task completion, efficiency, and safety violation penalties.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, end-to-end trainable architecture for safe TAMP that explicitly bridges high-level reasoning with low-level safety filters. It will provide a framework for scaling safety-certifiable control to complex, multi-step tasks, demonstrated in simulation on long-horizon mobile manipulation and navigation scenarios. The resulting system will enhance the interpretability and reliability of autonomous decision-making.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight, learnable integration of BarrierNet's differentiable safety layers within a hierarchical GNN-based planner, enabling end-to-end optimization of both task-level strategy and continuous safety constraints—a departure from prior work that treats safety as a separate, post-planning verification or low-level control module.

proposals.title: Causal Continuous-Time Dynamics for Interpretable and Efficient Robotic Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Current deep models for robotic control, such as those for manipulators, are often opaque black boxes and data-inefficient, requiring vast datasets for training. A causal, interpretable framework for modeling continuous-time agent dynamics is needed to improve sample efficiency, safety, and debugging in real-world robotic systems, aligning with pre-2026 pushes for trustworthy and efficient AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the causal continuous-time method, previously explored for autonomous driving, can be successfully adapted to model the dynamics of robotic manipulators, leading to more interpretable, data-efficient, and robust control policies.
proposals.method: We will adapt the causal continuous-time framework, likely based on Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (Neural ODEs) or structured state-space models, to model robotic manipulator dynamics. The method will be integrated with a variational autoencoder (VAE) to learn interpretable latent pathways, as in Rus's prior work, and trained using an efficient, loop-based control scheme on a curated dataset of manipulation trajectories. We will specifically test its ability to isolate causal factors like object properties and contact forces.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, interpretable dynamics model for robotic manipulators that requires less training data than standard end-to-end networks. It will provide a framework for diagnosing control failures and verifying system safety through its causal structure, advancing the field of trustworthy robotic learning.
proposals.novelty: While causal and continuous-time models exist, their application to learn interpretable, low-dimensional latent dynamics for high-DoF manipulators from raw sensory input is non-trivial and underexplored, moving beyond autonomous driving domains to a new, complex agent type.

proposals.title: Causal World Models for Sample-Efficient Reinforcement Learning in Autonomous Driving
proposals.motivation: Current reinforcement learning (RL) for autonomous driving suffers from poor sample efficiency and difficulty generalizing to novel scenarios, as it often learns correlations rather than causal structure. Imitation learning, while data-efficient, is limited by the coverage of the training dataset and cannot improve beyond it. Integrating causal representations, which disentangle the underlying factors of variation in driving scenes, with RL could enable agents to reason about interventions and adapt more robustly.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a causal world model, learned via a variational autoencoder (VAE) with structured latent pathways, with a model-based RL algorithm will yield a policy that is more sample-efficient, interpretable, and capable of safe generalization than end-to-end RL or pure imitation learning.
proposals.method: We will first train a causal VAE, building on Rus's prior work, to map high-dimensional sensory input (e.g., from LFM2) into a disentangled latent space where dimensions correspond to semantically meaningful, interventionally valid factors (e.g., actor presence, road geometry). This learned world model will then be integrated into a model-based RL loop (e.g., using Dreamer or PlaNet). The RL agent will learn a control policy by planning through the compact causal latent dynamics, enabling efficient exploration and reasoning about the consequences of actions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for sample-efficient, interpretable autonomous driving that moves beyond behavioral cloning. It will demonstrate how causal representations can ground model-based RL, leading to policies that are more robust to distribution shift and capable of safe, strategic reasoning in complex, dynamic environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of a semantically structured, causal VAE—designed for interpretability—as the core dynamics model for a model-based RL agent, moving beyond using VAEs solely for state representation or imitation. This combines the strengths of causal representation learning and long-horizon RL optimization.

proposals.title: Multi-Agent Graph Neural Networks for Robust Pursuit of Adversarial Targets
proposals.motivation: Autonomous systems in dynamic environments, such as multi-robot coordination or autonomous driving, must reason about complex, adversarial interactions with multiple moving targets. Pre-2026 methods often simplify these interactions to single-target pursuit or non-adversarial scenarios, limiting real-world applicability where targets may cooperate or act deceptively. This gap hinders the deployment of robust autonomous systems in security, logistics, and transportation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neural network architecture combining semantic-aware Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with latent pathway modeling can learn efficient, interpretable control policies for an agent to successfully pursue multiple, potentially adversarial, dynamic targets by explicitly reasoning about their relational structure and intent.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent GNN where nodes represent the ego-agent and dynamic targets, and edges encode their spatial and semantic relationships. The network's input function will integrate observed trajectories and semantic class (e.g., pedestrian, vehicle). A variational autoencoder (VAE) module, inspired by interpretability work, will learn a latent space of possible target strategies and future pathways. This latent representation will condition a control policy network, trained via model-free reinforcement learning within a closed-loop simulation, to generate efficient pursuit actions. Training will use a procedurally generated dataset of complex multi-target chase scenarios with adversarial objectives.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel neural control framework for multi-target adversarial pursuit, providing both performance gains and improved interpretability through latent strategy modeling. It will deliver a public simulation benchmark for complex dynamic interactions and advance the field of semantic-aware reasoning for autonomous systems under uncertainty.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of semantic GNNs for relational reasoning with a VAE-based latent strategy model specifically for adversarial multi-target pursuit, moving beyond single-target or non-adversarial control paradigms dominant in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Dual Adversarial Evasion: A Causal Multi-Agent Framework for Robustness in Autonomous Navigation
proposals.motivation: Autonomous systems like drones and self-driving cars must operate robustly in adversarial environments where other agents may act as unpredictable pursuers. Current single-agent reinforcement learning (RL) often fails to generalize to such dynamic, competitive scenarios. A principled framework for training an evader against an adaptive chaser is needed to stress-test and improve the robustness of autonomous control policies.
proposals.hypothesis: Training an evading agent and a chasing agent simultaneously within a shared causal world model will produce more robust and generalizable evasion policies than training against static or scripted opponents, by capturing the fundamental cause-effect relationships of the adversarial interaction.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-agent adversarial RL framework grounded in causal reasoning. Both the evader and chaser agents will be trained using an efficient model-based RL loop (e.g., DreamerV2) within a shared latent dynamics model—a structured variational autoencoder (VAE) that learns interpretable latent factors of the environment state. Their objectives are opposing: the evader maximizes time-to-capture, while the chaser minimizes it. We will use a multi-agent dataset of simulated pursuit-evasion trajectories to pre-train the world model, then fine-tune the adversarial policies.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel benchmark and training paradigm for adversarial robustness in autonomous navigation. It will yield insights into the failure modes of autonomous systems under pursuit and produce evasion policies that are more adaptable to novel chaser strategies. The shared causal model enhances interpretability of the adversarial dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the use of a single, shared causal world model to train both adversarial agents concurrently, forcing the emergence of robust strategies grounded in the environment's true dynamics, rather than through self-play on raw observations. This integrates Rus's prior work on interpretable latent models directly into a competitive multi-agent setting.

ideas.title: Continuous-Time State Estimation with Uncertainty-Aware Asynchronous Fusion
ideas.description: This system treats the underlying state (e.g., robot pose, object dynamics) as a continuous-time process, modeled with Gaussian Processes or neural ODEs, which provides a principled way to query the state at any arbitrary timestamp. Each asynchronous sensor measurement is fused by conditioning this continuous process, with the fusion weight inversely proportional to the accumulated uncertainty since the last update from that sensor modality. This approach directly addresses the core problem of information decay in gaps, preventing overconfidence in stale or extrapolated data and enabling robust performance even with highly irregular, sparse sampling rates across sensors.

ideas.title: Task-Aligned Self-Supervised BEV Pre-training via Cross-View Consistency and Spatial Reasoning
ideas.description: This approach pre-trains a unified BEV feature encoder using a self-supervised objective designed to instill task-relevant priors. The core pretext tasks enforce cross-view consistency between camera images and corresponding simulated sparse depth in BEV, and require the model to predict masked regions of dynamic objects and static structures within the BEV grid. This curriculum ensures the learned features inherently capture robust geometry, objectness, and scene layout. Consequently, the pre-trained encoder provides a stronger, more transferable initialization for diverse downstream BEV perception tasks like detection and map construction.

ideas.title: Dynamic, Condition-Aware Sensor Fusion with a Modality-Agnostic Core Representation
ideas.description: This system employs a central neural network that ingests raw or minimally processed data from cameras, LiDAR, and radar to build a unified scene representation. A conditioning network continuously analyzes the real-time signal quality and environmental context from each sensor stream to dynamically adjust the fusion weights. This allows the core perception model to rely on the most reliable sensors for any given feature or region under current conditions, seamlessly transitioning between sensor dominance. The result is a resilient system where perception degrades gracefully with sensor failures, rather than collapsing due to the primary sensor's weakness.

ideas.title: Adaptive BEV Grids via Computational Fluid Dynamics Mesh Refinement Techniques
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of fixed BEV grid resolution by drawing an analogy from Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), which solves the analogous problem of simulating complex flows efficiently using Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR). The analogous solution dynamically coarsens or refines the simulation mesh based on local solution variables like pressure gradients. Adapting this, we propose a BEV perception system where the grid resolution is not fixed but varies dynamically based on real-time scene content metrics (e.g., object density, semantic uncertainty, sensor data entropy). This creates a hierarchical, adaptive BEV representation that allocates computational resources precisely where needed for detailed understanding (e.g., near pedestrians) while maintaining efficiency in large, simple areas (e.g., open highway), thereby improving both the accuracy and efficiency of downstream perception and prediction tasks.

ideas.title: Temporally-Aware Sparse & Adaptive BEV Pooling for Efficient 4D Perception
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel BEV perception architecture that tightly couples temporal fusion with a fundamentally re-engineered pooling operation. Instead of applying dense, static BEV pooling to every frame, the method learns to predict and pool only from spatially and temporally salient regions across a 4D volume (3D space + time), guided by motion cues and feature entropy from previous frames. This enables efficient processing of long video sequences for robust temporal context, while the adaptive pooling mechanism—co-designed with hardware-aware kernels—dynamically balances accuracy and latency, making high-resolution, long-horizon 4D perception feasible for real-time autonomous systems.

ideas.title: Sparse Metric Anchoring for Scale-Accurate Camera-Centric BEV Perception
ideas.description: This approach maintains a camera-first architecture for dense 3D BEV perception but overcomes its fundamental metric inaccuracy by fusing sparse, high-confidence range measurements from a low-cost auxiliary sensor. A neural correction network uses these sparse metric anchors to continuously calibrate the monocular depth estimation, resolving scale ambiguity and ensuring globally consistent metric outputs. The system remains robust to temporary occlusion or dropout of the sparse range sensor by falling back to a temporally-fused, corrected camera-only mode. This results in a perception system that is both cost-effective and delivers the metric accuracy required for safe downstream planning and control.

ideas.title: Network-Aware Dynamic Pooling for Sparse 3D Perception
ideas.description: This research applies network theory to the internal feature communication within 3D perception models. Instead of modeling whales, we model feature vectors or scene elements as nodes in a dynamic graph, where edge strengths are based on learned attention or correlation. We then use this graph to inform a hardware-aware BEV pooling operation, dynamically sparsifying or densifying the pooling kernel and memory access patterns based on the graph's community structure and information flow. This results in a perception system that is both more computationally efficient and more semantically coherent, as pooling respects the latent information network of the scene rather than just its geometry.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Hierarchical BEV Pooling Inspired by Memory Paging
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to develop advanced, hardware-aware optimizations for Bird's Eye View (BEV) pooling by drawing an analogy from computer architecture's memory management solutions. The analogous problem—efficiently mapping a large virtual address space to physical memory—is solved using hierarchical page tables and a fast cache (the TLB). We adapt this solution by designing an adaptive, hierarchical BEV pooling mechanism. This system would intelligently cache aggregated BEV features for regions with high temporal or spatial locality (e.g., the ego-vehicle's immediate path), performing the full, costly pooling operation from multi-view image features only when necessary. This approach optimizes for the common case in autonomous driving, leading to significant reductions in latency and computational overhead for the BEV pooling operation, making it more suitable for deployment on resource-constrained embedded hardware.

ideas.title: Content-Adaptive BEV Lattice via Joint Policy Prediction and Feature Warping
ideas.description: This system eliminates the bootstrapping problem of adaptive BEV grids by predicting the grid structure and populating it with features in a single, efficient forward pass. A lightweight convolutional network processes multi-scale image features to predict a sparse set of 3D reference points and their associated local grid resolutions, forming a non-uniform BEV lattice. Image features are then warped onto this lattice using camera geometry and learned attention, creating a scene representation that is inherently high-resolution near predicted regions of interest and low-resolution elsewhere. This approach ensures computational resources are allocated content-adaptively from the outset, without iterative refinement or a separate pre-processing model.

ideas.description: This research develops a framework for learning robust acoustic communication models from small or unlabeled datasets by explicitly targeting the root cause of failure: reliance on unstable, spurious correlations. The core innovation is a joint learning process that disentangles invariant causal factors of communication from environmental and speaker-specific nuisances using principles of invariant risk minimization. The system is rigorously validated through controlled OOD benchmarks and adversarial simulation environments, ensuring the learned representations generalize reliably beyond the limited training context.

ideas.title: Sparse, Query-Adaptive Feature Selection for BEV Construction
ideas.description: This research proposes to replace static, dense BEV pooling with a dynamic, sparse alternative that fundamentally alters the efficiency paradigm. A lightweight network module learns to predict the importance of each image feature for each potential 3D BEV location, generating a sparse set of (feature, 3D-location) pairs for computation. This allows the pooling operation to skip over vast swaths of the canonical dense grid, focusing computation only on semantically or geometrically pertinent regions. The system is trained end-to-end, enabling it to learn task-aware sparsity patterns that maximize efficiency while preserving or improving downstream perception performance.

ideas.title: Self-Supervised Pre-Training of Unified BEV Encoders for Cross-Modal Animal Focal Follow
ideas.description: This research fuses self-supervised pre-training for Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) features with ethology's focal follow methodology to create a unified cross-modal encoder. The system is trained on continuous, unlabeled streams of egocentric video (providing a dynamic BEV perspective of an individual animal) and synchronized audio recordings of its vocalizations. Through self-supervised objectives like temporal consistency prediction and audio-visual correspondence learning, the model learns to generate rich BEV feature maps that inherently fuse spatial behavior trajectories with acoustic event contexts. This pre-trained encoder can then be efficiently fine-tuned for downstream tasks such as automated ethogram generation, vocalization-source attribution, and the discovery of novel behavioral-vocal interaction patterns in naturalistic settings.

ideas.title: Acoustic BEV: Hardware-Aware Neural Synthesis of Bio-Plausible Whale Calls for Real-Time Field Playback
ideas.description: This research fuses generative bio-acoustic modeling with hardware-aware neural network optimization to create a system for real-time, adaptive synthesis of whale codas. We treat the spectro-temporal structure of a coda as a 2D 'scene' in a Bird's-Eye View (BEV) representation, where the model must learn to generate plausible acoustic 'objects' (call elements). The core innovation is applying advanced, hardware-optimized BEV pooling techniques to the latent space of a generative model, enabling it to run efficiently on low-power, portable field computers. This allows researchers to generate and modify synthetic calls on-the-fly in response to live animal vocalizations, enabling unprecedented interactive playback experiments to probe communication rules.

ideas.title: Acoustic Self-Supervised Learning via Contrastive Predictive Coding
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that understanding acoustic communication requires large, labeled datasets by drawing an analogy from breakthroughs in Natural Language Processing. In NLP, self-supervised learning on unlabeled text (e.g., via masked language modeling) has successfully created models that understand language structure, which are then adapted with small labeled sets. The adapted solution applies this to acoustic data: develop a method for self-supervised pre-training on massive volumes of unlabeled audio by using a contrastive predictive coding objective, where a neural network learns to represent audio by predicting future or missing acoustic frames in a latent space. This creates a foundational model that captures the building blocks of communication, drastically reducing the need for labeled examples when fine-tuning for specific tasks like species call recognition, emotion detection, or speech understanding in low-resource languages.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Holistic Scene Representation for Continuous Signal Interpretation
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel computational architecture for interpreting complex, continuous signals such as bio-acoustic communication. It rejects the assumption that perception or communication must be discretized, instead using a content-adaptive, hierarchical BEV-like latent space whose resolution and structure are dynamically warped by the input signal's own energy and information density. This allows the model to learn representations that preserve the holistic, analog qualities of the source, enabling analysis of phenomena like whale song without imposing artificial combinatorial units. The core innovation is a co-evolution of signal and representation, where the 'grid' for understanding is not fixed but is sculpted by the continuous signal it aims to model.

ideas.title: Gracefully Degrading Sensor Fusion with Self-Supervised Calibration Integrity Monitoring
ideas.description: This research develops a fusion framework that explicitly acknowledges the theoretical limits of operating with unknown calibration. It combines a primary robust fusion algorithm with a parallel monitor that continuously estimates a confidence bound on possible calibration errors using consistency checks between sensor streams. If the estimated miscalibration risk exceeds a safety threshold derived from observability analysis, the system automatically and smoothly transitions to a degraded but provably stable operating mode using a minimal sensor set or a last-known-good calibration. This ensures system safety and functional continuity even when the core 'robust to miscalibration' assumption is violated.

ideas.title: Sensor Fusion via Adaptive Ensemble Filtering, Inspired by Immune System Tolerance
ideas.description: This research challenges the core assumption that sensor fusion requires precise, static calibration. Drawing an analogy from computational immunology, where the immune system maintains robust self-tolerance despite a dynamically changing 'self', we propose a novel fusion method. The analogous solution involves decentralized, adaptive filtering (like T-cell negative selection and tunable activation) to distinguish signal from noise amid internal variation. Adapted to sensor fusion, this leads to the idea of an adaptive ensemble filtering framework. The system maintains a population of sensor models with hypothesized calibration states. A meta-algorithm, mimicking immune system tolerance mechanisms, continuously evaluates the consensus and contextual plausibility of these models, dynamically adjusting their influence on the fused output. This creates a method inherently robust to both persistent miscalibration and dynamic calibration changes, as the system learns to 'tolerate' a range of sensor behaviors while fusing data effectively.

ideas.title: Multi-Modal Sensor Fusion for Decoding Animal Communication Syntax in 3D Behavioral Context
ideas.description: This research fuses the search for higher-level combinatorial rules in animal vocalizations with multi-sensor data integration into a unified 3D representation. The core idea is to create a multi-modal Bird's-Eye View (BEV) model that synchronously incorporates acoustic units, detailed 3D movement trajectories from radar/LiDAR, and environmental data to analyze animal groups. By treating coordinated physical maneuvers and spatial relationships as additional 'modalities' alongside vocal sequences, the system can identify whether specific syntactic vocal patterns are contingent upon or trigger particular 3D spatial behaviors, revealing the true functional grammar of communication embedded within its physical and social context.

ideas.title: Population-Grounded Adaptive Modeling of Individual Whale Vocal Signatures
ideas.description: This approach combines the power of population-level acoustic modeling with fine-grained individual adaptation. A foundational neural network is first trained on a broad dataset of whale vocalizations to learn species- and context-dependent acoustic features. For a target individual, a small, trainable adapter module is attached to this frozen foundation and tuned exclusively on that whale's data, creating a personalized model. The system continuously integrates new recordings via online learning techniques, allowing the individual signature model to adapt to natural vocalization drift over time while remaining robust due to its stable foundational knowledge.

ideas.title: Robust Discovery of Bioacoustic Syntax Under Sensor Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research direction develops novel signal fusion and sequence analysis methods that are explicitly robust to sensor miscalibration and dynamic calibration drift. By applying these methods to long-term, multi-sensor recordings of whale vocalizations, the approach can separate true combinatorial rules in the communication sequences from artifacts introduced by variable sensor sensitivity, frequency response, or orientation. This allows for the first reliable, large-scale searches for syntax-like patterns across disparate and imperfect real-world acoustic datasets, transforming noisy field recordings into a reliable resource for linguistic analysis. The core advance is a joint inference framework that simultaneously learns both the latent combinatorial structure of the vocalizations and the unknown, time-varying calibration parameters of the sensors used to record them.

ideas.title: Continuous-Time State Estimation for Asynchronous Sensors via Event-Based Filtering
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of synchronous sensor data by adapting solutions from astrophysics, where telescopes provide sporadic, asynchronous observations of celestial objects. The analogous solution uses continuous-time state estimation filters (like Kalman filters) to incorporate irregular measurements as they arrive, updating a running prediction. Adapted to sensor fusion, the research would develop an event-based filtering framework that maintains a continuous state estimate, fusing each asynchronous sensor reading at its precise timestamp without buffering or forced synchronization, thereby reducing latency and improving robustness in systems with variable sensor rates.

ideas.title: A Tiered Active Validation System for Crowdsourced Bioacoustic Data
ideas.description: This redesigned system crowdsources whale acoustic recordings while rigorously safeguarding data quality through an integrated, multi-tiered validation architecture. It employs an on-device filter to remove obvious noise upon capture, then routes potential whale vocalizations to a centralized pool. A benchmark machine learning model, continuously refined via active learning on expert-verified samples, automatically scores incoming submissions for likely validity. These scores, combined with trusted contributor validations, create a probabilistic quality label for each clip, allowing researchers to build datasets with known confidence metrics. The system directly uses uncertain samples to prompt expert review, creating a scalable, self-improving loop that ensures the growing dataset meets scientific standards.

ideas.title: Temporal BEVFusion for Crowdsourced Bioacoustic Monitoring
ideas.description: This research fuses citizen-science whale acoustic data collection with temporal BEVFusion principles to create a novel bioacoustic perception system. The core idea is to treat acoustic data from distributed, low-cost citizen sensors (e.g., hydrophones on sailing vessels) as asynchronous, multi-frame inputs to a temporal Bird's-Eye View (BEV) network. The system will learn to fuse these sparse, irregularly sampled audio clips across time and space into a coherent, dynamic model of whale presence, movement, and behavior. This enables robust population tracking and activity prediction over wide areas by computationally integrating imperfect, crowdsourced data streams, overcoming the limitations of single, high-fidelity sensor stations.

ideas.title: Adaptive Multi-Modal Tagging for Continuous Bio-Behavioral Tracking
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from marine biotelemetry, where animal-borne 'bio-logging' tags (like DTAGs) solve the problem of capturing synchronized movement and acoustic data from free-ranging individuals. The analogous solution involves a single, animal-mounted device that records continuous 3D kinematics via IMU sensors alongside audio from an on-board microphone, all on a common timebase. Adapted for terrestrial ethology, this becomes a 'focal-follow tag'—a wearable device that automates and quantifies the traditional focal follow by providing a continuous, synchronized stream of behavioral (movement, posture, activity) and vocalization data from the individual's perspective, enabling detailed analysis of the relationship between action and communication.

ideas.title: BEVFusion for Spatiotemporal Acoustic Unit Detection in Marine Mammal Communication
ideas.description: This research applies the multi-modal fusion principles of BEVFusion to the domain of bioacoustics, specifically for analyzing sperm whale codas. By fusing synchronized multi-hydrophone array audio with contextual 3D spatial data (e.g., from depth sensors or bathymetric maps), the model constructs a unified Bird's Eye View (BEV) representation of the underwater acoustic scene over time. This spatiotemporal BEV representation allows the model to not only identify fundamental acoustic units within clicks but also localize their source in 3D space and track their propagation and sequencing as the whales move. The outcome is a powerful tool for decoding the structure and potential meaning of whale communication by modeling it as a dynamic 3D perceptual task.

ideas.title: Hardware-Aware Unsupervised Acoustic Unit Pooling for Sparse Bioacoustic Data
ideas.description: This research fuses hardware-aware BEV pooling optimization with unsupervised speech unit discovery to create a novel pipeline for processing sparse, long-duration bioacoustic data like whale clicks. The core innovation is a learnable, hardware-optimized pooling operation that simultaneously performs acoustic unit discovery and compresses the irregular 3D point cloud of click events into a compact, discrete token sequence. This unified approach bypasses the need for separate feature extraction and clustering stages, producing a representation optimized for both downstream classification tasks and efficient deployment on edge computing hardware in remote monitoring buoys or autonomous underwater vehicles.

ideas.title: Information-Theoretic Grammar Induction for Coda Sequence Complexity
ideas.description: This research direction adapts grammar induction and dependency parsing algorithms to generate candidate syntactic models for coda sequences. It then applies information-theoretic measures, such as minimum description length (MDL) and predictive information, to evaluate and rank these induced grammars. The synergy enables the identification of the most efficient structural representation that balances model complexity with its ability to compress and predict the sequence. This provides a principled, quantitative method to move beyond qualitative structural analysis to a model selection framework that reveals the inherent computational complexity of coda communication systems.

ideas.title: Spatio-Temporal Verification Framework for Inferring Cetacean Communication Networks
ideas.description: This approach begins by deploying a coordinated hydrophone array to perform robust 3D localization and tracking of vocalizing whales, establishing verified 'acoustic agents' as network nodes. Communication edges are proposed not from simple call co-occurrence, but from statistically validated temporal patterns (e.g., matched intervals, overlapping call types) exchanged between tracked agents, controlling for environmental and chorus effects. The resulting network model is grounded in physically verified interactions, allowing for rigorous analysis of information flow structure, key individuals, and group coordination dynamics with minimized attribution error.

ideas.title: Asynchronous Multi-Sensor Fusion for Individual Whale Signature Tracking
ideas.description: This research fuses individual vocal signature modeling with asynchronous data handling to track a single target whale using a sparse, uncoordinated array of sensors (e.g., hydrophones, drones, tags). The core challenge is to identify the whale's unique signature across sensor streams that are not temporally aligned and may capture fragmented, non-overlapping audio or movement data. The novel ML framework will use the individual's signature as a spatial-temporal anchor, correlating asynchronous detections to reconstruct the whale's path and behavior over time and space, overcoming the limitations of traditional synchronized array processing.

ideas.title: Asynchronous Affective Signal Processing for Non-Referential Communication
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a framework for analyzing animal or human communicative interactions where the core signals (like clicks or vocalizations) are treated as having no external referential meaning, serving primarily affective or social functions. Crucially, it relaxes the requirement for synchronous data streams from different sensors (audio, video, physiological), allowing them to be processed asynchronously. The fusion creates a novel approach where the temporal *relationships* and *delays* between these asynchronous affective signals become the primary data, revealing patterns of emotional resonance, turn-taking, and social bonding that are invisible to models demanding strict alignment or searching for referential content. This enables the study of communication in noisy, naturalistic settings where signals are sparse, overlapping, and driven by internal state rather than external events.

ideas.title: Apply Epidemic Model Inference to Whale Vocalization Networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational epidemiology to the original context of modeling whales as nodes in an information network. In epidemiology, network-based SIR models are used to infer the source of disease outbreaks and transmission pathways from partial temporal data on infections. The analogous solution is adapted by conceptualizing novel whale vocalizations (e.g., a new song phrase) as an 'information contagion' spreading through the social-acoustic network. By applying inference algorithms from epidemic source detection to the temporal sequence of vocalization adoptions across recorded whales, the research can model the likely origin point of new calls, estimate social learning rates, and reconstruct the pathways of cultural transmission within cetacean societies, providing a dynamic model of information flow.

ideas.title: Disentangling the Co-evolutionary Dynamics of Communication and Social Structure
ideas.description: This research program rejects the simplistic 'which causes which' framing and instead maps the specific, testable causal mechanisms through which communication and social structure co-evolve. It uses longitudinal digital trace data to model their reciprocal influence over time, complemented by randomized interventions that manipulate communication affordances to observe effects on group formation and hierarchy. Finally, agent-based models are developed to explore how hypothesized mechanisms scale, providing a robust, mechanistic understanding of the dynamic system.

ideas.title: Causal Inference of Emergent Social Structure from Asynchronous Multi-Modal Traces
ideas.description: This research direction develops methods to infer whether and how social structures emerge from communication patterns by analyzing asynchronous, multi-modal sensor data (e.g., messaging timestamps, call logs, co-location Bluetooth pings, or forum activity). Instead of assuming a pre-existing social graph to explain communication, the approach treats the temporal and causal relationships within the messy, unsynchronized data streams as the primary evidence. The core innovation is a joint model that relaxes strict temporal alignment to handle real-world data lags while testing the causal hypothesis that specific patterns of interaction (like bursts of asynchronous replies or correlated activity across modalities) are predictive of and generative for later, sustained group affiliations.

ideas.title: Individual Vocal Ethograms: Constructing Idiosyncratic Communication Models via Continuous Audio-Focal Follows
ideas.description: This research direction combines continuous focal-animal follows with synchronized audio recording to build comprehensive, individual-specific vocal ethograms. By prioritizing the individual over the group, the method captures the full range, frequency, and contextual deployment of an individual's vocalizations alongside its concurrent non-vocal behavior. This longitudinal, high-density data allows for modeling the individual's idiosyncratic 'vocabulary' and syntactic rules, treating their communication not as a subset of a group code but as a personal signature system. The synergy reveals how an individual's unique communicative style is functionally integrated into its broader behavioral landscape, a question inaccessible to either group-level call analysis or behavioral tracking alone.

ideas.title: Inverse Social Acoustics: Synthesizing Communication to Probe and Remodel Social Structure
ideas.description: This research inverts the traditional view by treating social structure as an emergent property of communication, not its prerequisite. It applies the inverse problem framework from bioacoustics to learn generative models of communication (e.g., whale codas, primate calls) that capture the statistical patterns defining a group. The core experiment uses these models to generate and play back novel, 'out-of-distribution' synthetic signals to a social group, observing if exposure to these new communication patterns can induce measurable changes in alliance formation, interaction networks, or group cohesion, thereby directly testing causality.

ideas.title: Inferring Social Topology from Dynamic Communication Flows
ideas.description: This research inverts the classic sociological assumption that pre-existing social groups determine communication patterns. Drawing an analogy from network neuroscience, where functional communication is used to infer effective brain connectivity, we propose a study where digital communication traces (emails, chats, forum posts) are the primary input. Using generative network models from dynamic systems analysis, we infer the latent, causal social structure—the effective network of who influences whom—directly from the timing and flow of communication. The core challenge is to see if this inferred, communication-shaped structure is a stronger predictor of emergent social group boundaries, cohesion, and identity than traditional, demographically or formally defined groups, thereby positioning communication patterns as the architect rather than the occupant of social topology.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Bayesian Inference of Communication: Group Priors for Novelty, Individual Posteriors for Specificity
ideas.description: This research designs a model that posits communication as generated from a hierarchical structure: a shared, foundational group code forms a prior, from which individual styles deviate. For a new individual, interpretation relies on the general group prior, ensuring baseline functionality. As interaction data accumulates, the model incrementally updates to capture the individual's specific stylistic signature, smoothly blending general and personal understanding. This approach guarantees robustness to novelty while achieving the precision of individualized models where data permits.

ideas.title: Species-Specific Communication Hypothesis Space: A Priors-Driven Framework for Cross-Taxa Deciphering
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea inverts the original template approach. For any new species, the first step is to convene an expert panel spanning ethology, sensory biology, cognitive science, and ecology in a structured debate to define the 'Communication Hypothesis Space'—the set of biologically plausible communication modalities, functions, and complexities. This consensus document then actively constrains and informs the design of a customized data collection and analysis roadmap, ensuring tools and models are fit-for-purpose. The final output is not a single template, but a generative method for creating rigorous, species-appropriate research protocols that avoid the pitfalls of anthropomorphism or taxon-centric bias.

ideas.title: Syntax-Agnostic Functional Structure Discovery in Coda Sequences
ideas.description: This approach uses computational linguistics parsers not as definitive analyzers, but as a source of initial structural hypotheses for coda sequences. These hypothesized structures are then subjected to a validation framework that tests their predictive power against independent, non-linguistic data such as caller identity, behavioral context, or environmental variables. Structures that fail to show significant statistical or functional associations are rejected, preventing the imposition of human syntactic bias. The final output is a set of sequence patterns and dependencies empirically validated for their role within the specific cetacean communication system.

ideas.title: Decoding whale vocal syntax using computational linguistics methods
ideas.description: This research idea adapts solutions from computational linguistics to search for higher-level language structures in whale vocalizations. In computational linguistics, researchers successfully use statistical models (n-grams, hidden Markov models, probabilistic context-free grammars) and unsupervised learning to discover syntactic patterns and grammatical rules in human languages from unannotated text. By applying these same techniques to sequences of whale communication units, we can systematically search for combinatorial rules and syntax-like patterns without imposing human linguistic biases. The adapted approach would involve collecting extensive datasets of whale vocalization sequences, then using computational linguistic methods to automatically identify recurring patterns, potential hierarchical structures, and rule-governed combinations that might represent a form of syntax in whale communication.

ideas.title: Apply Lempel-Ziv Complexity from Dynamical Systems to Coda Wave Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from dynamical systems and chaos theory—specifically the use of Lempel-Ziv complexity for time-series analysis—to the problem of quantifying the complexity of seismic coda sequences. In the analogous field, LZ complexity is a well-established, model-free method to measure the information content and unpredictability of symbolic sequences derived from systems like brain activity or chaotic oscillators. The adapted research idea involves applying this same algorithmic approach to seismic coda waves: first symbolizing the coda time series, then calculating its LZ complexity as a direct measure of the medium's scattering heterogeneity and the sequence's predictive complexity. This offers a novel, information-theoretic metric for coda analysis that is complementary to physical models of attenuation and diffusion.

ideas.title: Apply seismic signal pattern discovery to whale click unit discovery
ideas.description: This idea adapts unsupervised pattern discovery methods from geophysics/seismology to the problem of finding acoustic units in whale clicks. In seismology, techniques like waveform cross-correlation, template matching, and clustering on seismic features are successfully used to discover repeated seismic events (e.g., earthquake families, tremors) within noisy, continuous data streams, without prior knowledge of event types. We propose to directly transfer this solution approach: process long-duration recordings of whale click sequences as continuous acoustic waveforms, apply similar cross-correlation and clustering pipelines to identify recurring, similar click patterns, and thereby discover a foundational inventory or 'dictionary' of distinct acoustic units. This method offers a robust, field-tested alternative to speech-inspired SSL for uncovering the basic building blocks of whale acoustic communication in an unsupervised manner.

ideas.title: Causal Acoustic Ecology: Experimentally Probing Whale Communication Dynamics within a Living Ocean Soundscape
ideas.description: This research moves beyond passive observation to actively test hypotheses about how the broader acoustic environment influences whale communication. By combining long-term, multi-species soundscape recordings with spatially and temporally targeted playback experiments, the system measures the direct behavioral and acoustic responses of whales to specific natural and anthropogenic sounds. This approach isolates the causal drivers of communication changes, such as masking, signal alteration, or behavioral shifts, within the complex acoustic network. The result is a mechanistic understanding of whale communication as an interactive process within a dynamic acoustic ecosystem, rather than a mere component of a soundscape catalog.

ideas.title: Modeling Individual Stylistic 'Dialects' via Population-Level Statistical Decomposition
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a dominant, shared group communication code by prioritizing the modeling of individual signature styles. It draws an analogy from population genetics, where techniques like PCA decompose total genetic variation to separate population structure from individual-specific patterns. The adapted solution applies similar statistical decomposition methods (e.g., ICA, VAEs) to communication data, modeling the shared system as a latent background from which individual stylistic 'dialects' systematically diverge. This reframes the research focus to quantify and characterize individual variation as the dominant, structured signal rather than noise around a group norm.
